hints.text: A 20-year-old male university rower presents with 72 hours of fever (maximum 39.2°C), sore throat and acute onset bilateral conjunctival injection with watery discharge. He reports photophobia and odynophagia; on examination he has tender, mobile anterior cervical lymphadenopathy and injected conjunctiva without purulent crusting. He has missed two training sessions because of malaise and eye pain.

Previously healthy, no chronic medications, no known immunodeficiency and up to date with routine vaccinations. He returned 4 days ago from a weekend regatta where teammates slept in shared accommodation and used the team’s chlorinated cooling pool between races; several teammates developed sore throat and red eyes beginning 48 hours after the event.

Blood tests: white blood cell count 7.8 × 10^9/L with neutrophils 5.0 × 10^9/L and lymphocytes 2.3 × 10^9/L; C-reactive protein 28 mg/L. Rapid antigen test for group A streptococcus from a throat swab negative. Bacterial cultures from conjunctival swabs show no growth; cytology of conjunctival aspirate shows a lymphocyte-predominant inflammatory infiltrate.
Chest radiograph is clear without focal consolidation. Neck ultrasound demonstrates multiple small (≤ 1.5 cm) reactive lymph nodes in the anterior cervical chain without abscess or necrosis.

Multiplex PCR performed on conjunctival and throat swabs (returned within 24 hours) detected a non-enveloped, double-stranded DNA respiratory virus known to cause outbreaks of pharyngitis with conjunctivitis among close-contact groups and after exposure at communal swimming facilities; identical PCR results were obtained from three teammates.

explanation: The presentation of acute fever, pharyngitis and bilateral conjunctivitis in a cluster of close-contact athletes after a regatta, together with negative bacterial cultures and a PCR positive for a non-enveloped dsDNA respiratory virus, indicates infection with adenovirus causing a pharyngoconjunctival syndrome. Diagnosis is supported by rapid onset among teammates and conjunctival/throat PCR concordance. Management in an immunocompetent young adult is supportive (analgesia, hydration, topical lubricants for eyes, infection control measures); antibiotics are not indicated. In immunocompromised hosts or severe pneumonia, antiviral options and specialist input may be required.

hints.text: A 24-year-old woman presents to a regional clinic with five days of crampy periumbilical abdominal pain, progressive abdominal distension and bilious vomiting. She reports reduced stool output but no overt gastrointestinal bleeding. On examination she is afebrile, mildly tachycardic, with abdominal distension, visible peristalsis and diffuse mild tenderness without rebound; bowel sounds are hyperactive.

She grew up and lives in a farming village and works harvesting leafy vegetables. She recalls using untreated human waste as fertiliser on a family plot until adolescence. No chronic medical conditions, not pregnant, and she has never received regular deworming. She notes long-standing poor appetite and has lost 4 kg over the past year.

Full blood count shows haemoglobin 95 g/L, white blood cell count 14.2 × 10^9/L with eosinophils 2.6 × 10^9/L. C-reactive protein 8 mg/L. A fresh stool wet mount prepared in clinic later that day demonstrates numerous large, oval eggs measuring approximately 50–75 µm with a thick, variably mamillated outer layer.

Plain abdominal radiograph demonstrates multiple dilated loops of small bowel with air–fluid levels. Focused abdominal ultrasound shows several long, tubular, echogenic structures within the jejunal lumen with a central anechoic tract; some structures show gentle movement with peristalsis.

During observation the patient passes a pale, cylindrical, mobile intact specimen in her stool measuring approximately 20 cm in length with tapered ends; the specimen is photographed and submitted for identification.

explanation: This presentation—intestinal obstruction in a young adult from a rural, agriculture-based setting with a history of exposure to untreated human faeces, microcytic anaemia, marked eosinophilia, stool microscopy showing large mammillated eggs ~50–75 µm, imaging revealing tubular intraluminal structures, and passage of a long pale cylindrical helminth—is diagnostic of infection with Ascaris lumbricoides. First-line therapy for uncomplicated intestinal infection is oral benzimidazole (albendazole or mebendazole); obstructive complications may require nasogastric decompression and surgical evaluation.

hints.text: A 34-year-old male artisanal leatherworker presents with a 4-day history of a single lesion on the dorsal aspect of his right forearm. He describes a small pruritic papule that enlarged and ulcerated; on examination there is a 3-cm central black eschar with a surrounding tense, non-pitting oedema extending well beyond the margins of the lesion. He has a low-grade fever of 38.2°C and a tender ipsilateral axillary lymph node; distal perfusion and motor function are preserved.

Previously well, no diabetes or chronic lung disease, not taking immunosuppressive medications, HIV test last year was negative. He did not receive any specific occupational vaccination. The lesion progressed rapidly over 48–72 hours despite application of over-the-counter topical antibiotic ointment; he reports no prior antibiotic prescription for this episode.

Blood tests: white cell count 14.6 × 10^9/L with neutrophils 11.8 × 10^9/L, CRP 86 mg/L. Material scraped from the lesion sent for microscopy shows large Gram-positive bacilli arranged singly and in short chains. After 24 hours on blood agar the wound culture grew large, grey, non-haemolytic colonies; capsule visualisation was reported on wet-mount preparation.

Soft-tissue ultrasound of the forearm demonstrates marked subcutaneous oedema surrounding the ulcer without a discrete fluid collection; there is no gas in the soft tissues and plain radiograph shows no bony involvement.

On directed questioning he recalls trimming and handling several unprocessed goat hides at the small tannery where he works three days before the lesion appeared; he sustained a small abrasion on that forearm while handling the raw material. Two co-workers subsequently developed similar skin lesions and the tannery had recently imported hides from a rural region where livestock had been reported to die unexpectedly.

explanation: The combination of a painless black eschar with extensive non-pitting surrounding oedema, rapid progression from papule to ulcer, occupational contact with unprocessed animal hides, and identification of large Gram-positive, non-haemolytic bacilli with a capsule on culture is most consistent with Bacillus anthracis causing cutaneous infection. Management includes prompt targeted antimicrobial therapy (for example a fluoroquinolone or doxycycline in adults) and notification to public-health authorities because of the zoonotic and public-health implications; antitoxin and escalation of care are considered for systemic disease.

hints.text: A 24-year-old man presented to a community clinic with abrupt-onset profuse vomiting that began approximately 3 hours after a catered business lunch; he reported 12 episodes over 6 hours, light-headedness and one episode of near-syncope. He denied diarrhoea, fever or bloody stools. On arrival he was tachycardic (110 beats/min), blood pressure 95/60 mmHg and appeared dry with decreased skin turgor; abdominal examination was soft and non-tender.

Previously healthy, no regular medications, no recent antibiotics and no chronic illness. He ate several dishes at the luncheon and sat with colleagues; three coworkers developed similar vomiting within a comparable interval. He had not travelled recently and had no known immunosuppression.

Laboratory tests: white blood cell count 8.5 × 10^9/L, C-reactive protein 3 mg/L, sodium 137 mmol/L, potassium 3.8 mmol/L. A stool specimen showed no fecal leukocytes; Gram stain of the stool specimen demonstrated large, boxy Gram-positive rods, some with refractile spores. Blood cultures remained sterile after 48 hours.

Contrast-enhanced CT abdomen performed to exclude surgical causes showed no bowel-wall thickening, no focal inflammatory changes, no obstruction and no free intraperitoneal air or fluid; overall appearance argued against invasive enteritis.

Public-health follow-up found the buffet included a rice-based fried dish that had been prepared the previous evening, left at room temperature, then briefly reheated before the luncheon. A sample of the leftover fried rice cultured a rapidly growing, beta-haemolytic, spore-forming Gram-positive rod matching the organism seen in the patient’s stool; multiple attendees who ate that dish reported similar short-interval vomiting-only illness.

explanation: The combination of very short incubation (≈1–6 hours), predominant vomiting without inflammatory diarrhoea, rapid improvement with supportive care, stool microscopy showing large, spore-forming Gram-positive rods, and a common exposure to reheated rice strongly points to the foodborne emetic illness caused by Bacillus cereus. CT showed no enteritis, supporting a toxin-mediated process rather than invasive infection. Management is primarily fluid resuscitation and antiemetics; antibiotics are not indicated for the typical emetic syndrome. Public-health measures include identifying and removing contaminated food and reviewing safe cooling/reheating practices to prevent spore germination and toxin production.

hints.text: A 24-year-old man presents to the clinic with 48 hours of profuse, crampy lower abdominal pain and 8–10 loose bowel motions per day that have become streaked with blood. He reports subjective fevers and two episodes of non‑bilious vomiting. On examination he is febrile 38.5 °C, pulse 110/min, mildly dehydrated, and tender in the right lower quadrant without peritonism.

Previously healthy, no regular medications and no recent antibiotics. He is a graduate student and ate at a backyard barbecue 3 days before symptom onset; he recalls eating undercooked poultry. No recent travel outside the country, no sick household contacts, and no immunosuppression.

Blood tests show leukocytosis 14.2 × 10^9/L with neutrophil predominance and CRP 76 mg/L. Serum potassium 3.2 mmol/L. Stool microscopy demonstrates numerous fecal leukocytes and RBCs; routine stool culture and PCR panel are pending.

Contrast-enhanced CT abdomen performed to evaluate focal tenderness shows segmental mural thickening and mucosal hyperenhancement of the terminal ileum and adjacent ascending colon with mild pericolic fat stranding, consistent with acute ileocolitis.

He was started on oral azithromycin after fluid resuscitation and potassium replacement; diarrhoea and pain improved substantially within 48 hours. Final microbiology report from stool culture: motile, curved Gram‑negative rods, oxidase positive, with characteristic darting motility on wet prep; isolates grew optimally at 42 °C and produced a positive hippurate hydrolysis test.

explanation: This presentation—acute onset bloody diarrhoea with fever and prominent right‑sided ileocolitis after eating undercooked poultry, leukocytosis and fecal leukocytes—followed by rapid clinical response to a macrolide and stool culture showing motile, curved, oxidase‑positive Gram‑negative rods that grow at 42 °C and are hippurate‑positive is diagnostic for Campylobacter jejuni. Management in uncomplicated cases focuses on hydration and electrolyte correction; macrolide therapy accelerates recovery and shortens shedding in symptomatic patients.

hints.text: A 23-year-old woman presents to a sexual health clinic with a 10-day history of mucopurulent vaginal discharge, mild dysuria, and intermenstrual spotting. She reports lower abdominal discomfort but no high fever or vomiting. On pelvic examination her cervix is friable with a mucopurulent endocervical discharge and mild cervical motion tenderness; she is using combined oral contraception and is not pregnant.

She reports a new male sexual partner four weeks earlier and inconsistent condom use since then. No history of intravenous drug use. She has one prior treated sexually transmitted infection five years ago. She is HIV-negative on recent testing and otherwise healthy.

Endocervical swab Gram stain shows abundant polymorphonuclear leukocytes but no intracellular Gram-negative diplococci; culture for Neisseria gonorrhoeae is negative. Nucleic acid amplification testing (NAAT) on the endocervical specimen is positive for the targeted genital pathogen. Full blood count shows WBC 7.8 × 10^9/L and C-reactive protein 11 mg/L; urine pregnancy test negative.

Transvaginal ultrasound demonstrates normal-appearing ovaries without adnexal masses and only small volume pelvic free fluid; no tubo-ovarian abscess is seen. The scan is otherwise unremarkable, supporting a diagnosis limited to the cervix/upper genital tract rather than advanced pelvic abscess.

Her recent male partner experienced two days of clear urethral discharge and dysuria shortly after their trip abroad; his urine NAAT returned positive for the same target sequence detected in her endocervical swab. Both were offered single-dose oral macrolide therapy and partner notification was arranged.

explanation: Young, sexually active patient with mucopurulent cervicitis, abundant neutrophils on Gram stain without visible intracellular Gram-negative diplococci, negative gonococcal culture, and a positive NAAT is most consistent with infection by Chlamydia trachomatis. The partner's symptomatic urethritis with a concordant positive urine NAAT and clinical improvement after single-dose macrolide therapy support this diagnosis. Transvaginal ultrasound excludes complicated pelvic abscess, keeping the presentation within uncomplicated cervicitis/early pelvic inflammatory spectrum.

hints.text: A 7-year-old girl is brought to a community clinic with a 3-week history of worsening cough. Parents describe sudden, forceful coughing fits—often at night—followed by an inspiratory 'whoop' and episodes of post-tussive vomiting; she has been afebrile except for a single low-grade temperature. Between paroxysms her respiratory exam is unremarkable and oxygen saturation is 97% on room air. Parents note one recent spell with brief perioral cyanosis.

Child is previously healthy. She completed the primary infant immunization series but missed the scheduled preschool booster at age 4 because of vaccine hesitancy. No chronic lung or cardiac disease, no recent travel. There is a 6-week-old sibling at home who is breastfed.

Full blood count: leukocytes 24.5 × 10^9/L with neutrophils 8.2 × 10^9/L and lymphocytes 14.6 × 10^9/L; C-reactive protein 4 mg/L. A multiplex respiratory viral PCR panel (influenza, RSV, adenovirus, rhinovirus) is negative. A nasopharyngeal swab has been sent for targeted PCR and culture for a fastidious respiratory pathogen.
Frontal chest radiograph shows perihilar peribronchial cuffing and small areas of subsegmental atelectasis centrally; no lobar consolidation or pleural effusion.

Local public-health follow-up identifies a cluster of similar prolonged-cough illness at the child’s daycare. The 6-week-old sibling was admitted overnight for apnoea and had a positive nasopharyngeal PCR identifying a fastidious, aerobic, gram-negative coccobacillary respiratory pathogen; household contacts were offered macrolide prophylaxis.

explanation: The presentation of prolonged paroxysmal nocturnal cough with inspiratory whoop, posttussive vomiting, marked lymphocytosis, incomplete booster immunization, a household infant with apnoea and a positive nasopharyngeal PCR, and a daycare cluster points to Bordetella pertussis. Diagnosis is supported by PCR from a nasopharyngeal specimen; early macrolide therapy reduces transmission and is recommended for cases and close contacts, especially to protect young infants.

hints.text: A 36-year-old woman presents to primary care with a 7-day history of an enlarging, non‑pruritic, erythematous rash on her right thigh. The lesion measures approximately 12 cm in diameter with central pallor and a well-demarcated outer border; she also reports mild headache and fatigue but no high fever or severe pain.

Two weeks earlier she spent a week camping in a wooded area in New England; she recalls finding and removing an engorged tick from the same thigh about 10 days before the rash appeared. No significant past medical history, not taking immunosuppressants, and no recent travel outside the region.

Initial blood tests show WBC 6.8 × 10^9/L, haemoglobin 136 g/L, platelets 260 × 10^9/L, CRP 9 mg/L. Blood cultures remain negative at 48 hours. A serum antibody test performed on day 7 after rash onset is negative for specific IgG and IgM.

Three days after presentation she develops right-sided lower motor‑neuron facial weakness. Non-contrast CT head is unremarkable; MRI brain with contrast shows no acute infarct, mass lesion, or enhancement to explain the palsy.

She is started empirically on oral doxycycline 100 mg twice daily. The rash fades substantially within one week of therapy and systemic symptoms remit; the facial palsy begins to improve over the following three weeks.

explanation: The clinical picture — an expanding annular rash with central clearing at the site of a recent tick bite, a short incubation period, subsequent cranial nerve VII palsy, early negative routine serology, and rapid clinical improvement after doxycycline — is most consistent with infection caused by Borrelia burgdorferi (early Lyme borreliosis). Early antibody tests can be negative, and clinical diagnosis with prompt antibiotic treatment is appropriate when the characteristic rash and exposure history are present.

hints.text: A 42-year-old man presents to the emergency department with an 18-hour history of progressive double vision, slurred speech and difficulty swallowing. He reports dry mouth and blurred vision; leg weakness developed later the same day. He is afebrile, pulse 88/min, respiratory rate 10/min, SpO2 93% on room air. Mental status is normal; sensory examination is intact, and cranial nerve testing demonstrates bilateral ptosis and impaired extraocular movements.

Previously healthy, non-smoker, no regular medications and no recent travel. He denies fever, diarrhoea or recent respiratory infection. Symptoms began abruptly and progressed during the day from visual disturbance to bulbar weakness; he has no history of myasthenia gravis or Guillain–Barré syndrome. There is no recent wound, injection or surgery.

Arterial blood gas on room air: pH 7.32, PaCO2 7.2 kPa, PaO2 8.1 kPa. Full blood count: white blood cells 8.9 × 10^9/L. C-reactive protein 2 mg/L. Serum electrolytes and renal function within reference ranges. Routine microbiology from blood cultures pending.

Non-contrast CT head is normal, with no acute intracranial process to explain cranial neuropathies. Chest radiograph shows low lung volumes with mild bibasilar atelectatic changes but no focal consolidation.

On further questioning he reports a family dinner two evenings earlier; he and two relatives ate home-canned green beans from an unrefrigerated jar. Both relatives developed transient double vision and dry mouth within 12–36 hours. Public-health testing on the leftover jar detected a heat-labile presynaptic neurotoxin; antitoxin and supportive respiratory care were arranged for the patient.

explanation: The presentation of acute, afebrile, symmetric cranial nerve dysfunction with dry mouth, descending weakness and early hypoventilation in the setting of a shared meal of inadequately preserved home-canned vegetables and detection of a heat-labile presynaptic neurotoxin indicates foodborne poisoning by Clostridium botulinum (preformed neurotoxin). Diagnostic clues are rapid onset after ingestion, normal sensorium and inflammatory markers, normal brain imaging, and a household cluster; management centers on antitoxin and respiratory support.

hints.text: A 7-year-old girl is brought to the emergency department with 48 hours of worsening sore throat, difficulty swallowing, increasing drooling and a hoarse voice. Her parents report low-grade fever and lethargy. On examination she has an adherent greyish membrane over both tonsils and the posterior pharynx that bleeds when gently scraped, tender anterior cervical lymphadenopathy, and a muffled voice with mild inspiratory stridor; oxygen saturation 96% on room air.

Family declines routine childhood immunisations; vaccination record shows the child did not receive primary infant doses. No chronic illness. Two weeks before presentation an adult household contact returned from extended travel abroad and developed a sore throat but did not seek medical care.

Throat swab Gram stain shows pleomorphic, club-shaped Gram-positive bacilli arranged in angular palisades. Methylene-blue stain highlights metachromatic granules. Peripheral blood shows WBC 15.8 × 10^9/L and CRP 84 mg/L. Culture on selective agar produced grey–black colonies after 48 hours.

Lateral neck radiograph demonstrates thickening of the oropharyngeal and hypopharyngeal soft tissues with narrowing of the upper airway at the subglottic level; chest radiograph shows clear lung fields without consolidation.

During contact tracing the symptomatic adult household contact was evaluated: their throat swab grew the same pleomorphic Gram‑positive bacillus and molecular testing detected an exotoxin-encoding gene. Public-health investigators note recent cases in the traveller’s region; the child had not received routine childhood immunisations that would have conferred protection.

explanation: The combination of an adherent grey pharyngeal membrane with bleeding on removal, airway narrowing, pleomorphic club-shaped Gram-positive bacilli on smear that form grey–black colonies on selective tellurite-containing media, and a tox gene-positive isolate in an unvaccinated child points to Corynebacterium diphtheriae producing an exotoxin. Management priorities include securing the airway, administering diphtheria antitoxin, and starting appropriate antibiotics (for example erythromycin or penicillin) while notifying public-health authorities for contact management and immunisation of susceptible contacts.

hints.text: A 3-year-old girl is brought to clinic with 48 hours of fever to 38.9°C, progressive refusal to eat and drooling. She is irritable but breathing comfortably. On inspection there are multiple shallow 2–4 mm vesicles and small shallow ulcers on the anterior tongue and buccal mucosa; clustered erythematous macules with tiny central vesicles are present on the palms and plantar surfaces. Urine output has fallen over the past day.

Previously healthy, up-to-date with routine immunisations, no chronic medications. Attends a local daycare; two classmates developed fever and mouth sores last week. No recent travel, no new foods, no insect bites. Older sibling had a similar but milder illness and recovered in 3–4 days.

Basic labs show WBC 7.8 × 10^9/L with a lymphocyte count 5.2 × 10^9/L, CRP 10 mg/L, sodium 136 mmol/L. Rapid strep test negative; bacterial throat culture pending. Point-of-care glucose normal. Clinical staff sent a throat swab for multiplex viral PCR and a stool sample for viral testing.

Close-up clinical photographs (oral and palmar) document multiple shallow grey-white vesicles on the anterior oral mucosa and clustered 2–3 mm clear vesicles on erythematous bases across the fingertips and palms; no signs of deeper tissue involvement on inspection and no cardiopulmonary findings on chest radiograph.

Public-health follow-up confirmed a daycare cluster with the same oral and acral lesions. Stool PCR returned positive for a small, non-enveloped, positive-sense RNA virus in the Picornaviridae family; sequence typing matched A-group strains most frequently reported in childhood oral and acral vesicular outbreaks.

explanation: The clinical picture—febrile child with acute painful oral ulcers and vesicular lesions on the hands/feet, daycare clustering, and stool PCR identifying a Picornaviridae A-group strain—is diagnostic for infection by Coxsackievirus (A serotypes), the common cause of childhood hand–foot–and–mouth disease and herpangina. Management is supportive (analgesia, hydration); recognition is important for infection control in group childcare settings.

hints.text: A 27-year-old woman presents to urgent care with 7 days of watery, non-bloody diarrhoea (6–10 stools/day), crampy lower abdominal pain and lightheadedness when standing. She reports poor sleep and reduced oral intake; temperature is 37.2°C, pulse 108/min and BP 100/60 mmHg with a 15 mmHg orthostatic drop. No recent antibiotic use. She cares for toddlers at a community daycare.

Past medical history notable only for treated childhood asthma. HIV test two years ago was negative; she is not immunosuppressed and takes no chronic medications. Ten days before symptom onset she spent a weekend at a public municipal swimming pool and remembers several children at the pool had diarrhoea; she also changed nappies at the daycare last week for a toddler with loose stools.

Because diarrhoea persisted despite oral rehydration, an abdominal CT with contrast was obtained: mild diffuse small-bowel and colonic wall oedema without focal fluid collection or obstruction, reported as enterocolitis.

She received intravenous fluids in clinic and a 5‑day course of oral ciprofloxacin was started empirically for presumed bacterial enteritis; there was no improvement after 72 hours. After supportive care plus initiation of an antiparasitic agent later that week, stool frequency decreased and she was back to baseline by day 5 of that therapy.

Stool testing: routine bacterial culture negative; modified acid-fast (modified Ziehl–Neelsen) stain demonstrated numerous spherical acid-fast oocysts measuring about 4–6 µm. A stool nucleic acid amplification test was positive for a small coccidian protozoan.

explanation: Profuse watery diarrhoea in an otherwise healthy adult with daycare and recent public-pool exposures, failure to respond to empiric antibacterial therapy, and identification of 4–6 µm acid-fast oocysts on stool stain strongly indicate infection with Cryptosporidium species. Resolution after institution of nitazoxanide and supportive care in an immunocompetent host is typical.

hints.text: A 32-year-old man presents with a 10-day history of progressively bloody diarrhoea, tenesmus and crampy lower abdominal pain. He returned two weeks earlier from a three-week stay in rural Peru. He reports 6–8 loose stools daily now streaked with bright red blood, intermittent low-grade fever; temperature 38.2 °C, pulse 102/min, mild dehydration, and tenderness in the lower abdomen without guarding.

Previously well, no chronic illnesses, not on corticosteroids or other immunosuppressants, no recent antibiotics or hospital admissions. He denies recent seafood ingestion or antibiotic use and has no sick contacts. HIV status unknown but no history of opportunistic infections.

Bloods: haemoglobin 125 g/L, leucocyte count 12.4 × 10^9/L, CRP 58 mg/L. Stool guaiac positive. Direct stool microscopy (wet mount) demonstrates motile protozoal forms that contain intact erythrocytes within the organisms. PCR panel for common bacterial enteric pathogens and routine stool culture are negative.

Contrast-enhanced CT abdomen shows a solitary 6.5-cm hypodense lesion in the right hepatic lobe with peripheral rim enhancement and surrounding hepatic oedema; no biliary dilatation. Ultrasound-guided aspiration was arranged.

On further questioning he recalls drinking untreated well and river water while in village communities. Ultrasound-guided aspiration yielded thick brown, odourless material; routine aerobic and anaerobic bacterial cultures remained sterile. Serology directed at invasive intestinal protozoa returned strongly positive for IgG; he improved clinically after a course of a nitroimidazole followed by a luminal cysticidal agent with interval shrinkage of the liver lesion.

explanation: The combination of recent travel to an endemic area, bloody colitis with stool microscopy showing motile protozoal forms that have ingested red blood cells, and a solitary right‑lobe liver abscess producing sterile brown pus is characteristic of infection by Entamoeba histolytica. Diagnostic clues here are erythrophagocytic trophozoites in stool and sterile aspirate from an amebic liver abscess. Recommended therapy is a tissue-active agent (eg, a nitroimidazole) to treat invasive disease followed by a luminal cysticidal agent (eg, paromomycin or diloxanide furoate) to eradicate intestinal carriage and prevent relapse.

hints.text: A 6-year-old girl is brought to a community paediatric clinic with a 3-week history of intense perianal itching that is worst at night and has disrupted sleep; parents report new daytime irritability and a few excoriations around the anus. No fever, no vomiting, and bowel habits are unchanged.

Child attends a local day-care. Parents note she often sucks her thumb and bites her nails. No recent travel, no chronic medical conditions, and immunisations are up to date. Her older brother had similar pruritus last month and was treated empirically by the family doctor.
Because of intermittent lower abdominal discomfort the clinician ordered an abdominal ultrasound; it was normal with no appendiceal abnormality, no masses, and no free fluid.

On further questioning, the mother reports that two other children in the day-care complained of nighttime perianal itching this term and that the family often shares bedding and towels. Multiple household members have noticed new perineal scratching.

A morning perianal adhesive-tape test applied to the perianal skin before bathing yields multiple ovoid, slightly flattened eggs ~50 × 30 µm on microscopy; during the exam a slender, white, thread-like adult about 1 cm long was seen on the perianal skin.

explanation: Findings are diagnostic of infection with a small, thread-like enteric nematode commonly causing nocturnal perianal pruritus in children. The morning adhesive-tape test demonstrating ovoid, flattened eggs and occasional visualization of the adult on perianal skin confirm the diagnosis. Management includes single-dose oral anthelmintic therapy (with a repeat dose after two weeks to interrupt reinfection), simultaneous treatment of household contacts, and strict hygiene measures (morning bathing, handwashing, laundering bedding and towels) to prevent autoinfection and household spread.

hints.text: An 82-year-old man resident in a long-term care facility presents with 48 hours of fever, new-onset confusion and dysuria. Staff report suprapubic tenderness on brief examination; temperature 38.7°C, heart rate 102/min, blood pressure 132/78 mmHg. A urinary catheter had been placed after urinary retention three days before symptom onset.

Medical history notable for benign prostatic hyperplasia, type 2 diabetes mellitus (well controlled on metformin), and osteoarthritis. He was hospitalised two weeks ago for a hip fracture repair and received a 5-day course of a third-generation cephalosporin perioperatively. No recent travel, no indwelling central lines, and no known allergies.

Bloods: white cell count 14.2 × 10^9/L, C-reactive protein 95 mg/L. Urinalysis: strongly positive leucocyte esterase, nitrite negative. Urine microscopy: >50 leucocytes/HPF and occasional epithelial cells. Gram stain of centrifuged urine shows Gram-positive cocci in short chains.

Bladder ultrasound performed at the bedside shows a post-void residual of approximately 260 mL and a diffusely thickened bladder wall without hydronephrosis. No perinephric collections identified on limited renal ultrasound.

Midstream urine and catheter specimen cultures grew grey, non‑hemolytic colonies. The isolate hydrolysed esculin in the presence of bile, grew in 6.5% NaCl, and was pyrrolidonyl arylamidase (PYR) positive. Antimicrobial testing showed resistance to many third‑generation cephalosporins but susceptibility to ampicillin; the patient was started on intravenous ampicillin and had defervescence and clinical improvement within 48 hours.

explanation: The clinical picture is catheter-associated urinary tract infection in an elderly patient with prior exposure to a third‑generation cephalosporin. Microscopy showing Gram‑positive cocci in short chains plus bile‑esculin hydrolysis, growth in 6.5% NaCl and PYR positivity identify the organism as Enterococcus faecalis. The susceptibility pattern (resistant to many cephalosporins but susceptible to ampicillin) and rapid clinical response to ampicillin support this diagnosis.

hints.text: A 19-year-old university student presents to the campus health clinic with 10 days of progressive sore throat, subjective fevers (measured up to 38.5°C), profound fatigue that limits activities, and painful posterior cervical lymph node swelling. On exam she has markedly tender posterior cervical and submandibular lymphadenopathy, bilateral tonsillar erythema with white exudates, and mild pharyngeal petechiae; there is no cough and no respiratory distress.

Symptoms began insidiously after a week of malaise. She saw an urgent-care clinician 3 days ago and was given oral amoxicillin for presumed bacterial pharyngitis. She reports a new intimate partner and multiple occasions of close mouth-to-mouth contact at a party approximately three weeks before onset. No chronic illnesses, no immunosuppressive medications, and no recent travel.

Full blood count: WBC 16.8 × 10^9/L with lymphocyte predominance (60%) and 22% atypical lymphocytes on peripheral smear. Alanine aminotransferase 138 U/L, C-reactive protein 18 mg/L. Rapid antigen test for group A streptococcus negative. Heterophile agglutination test returned positive.
Abdominal ultrasound performed because of left upper-quadrant tenderness shows splenic enlargement with a longitudinal length of 14.5 cm without focal lesions; chest radiograph is unremarkable.

Within 48 hours of starting amoxicillin the patient developed a diffuse pruritic maculopapular rash across the trunk and extremities; pharyngeal pain improved slowly with supportive care and rest, but fatigue and lymphadenopathy persisted. She was advised to avoid contact sports while splenomegaly resolves.

explanation: Young adult with prolonged fever, exudative pharyngitis, posterior cervical lymphadenopathy, atypical lymphocytosis, mildly elevated transaminases, splenomegaly, a positive heterophile test, and a generalized maculopapular rash after amoxicillin—findings consistent with acute infection due to Epstein-Barr virus causing infectious mononucleosis.

hints.text: A 28-year-old woman presents to urgent care with 48 hours of dysuria, urinary frequency, and suprapubic pressure. She reports a single episode of fever measured at home of 38.0 °C, denies flank pain, vaginal discharge, or haematuria. On exam she is alert, temperature 37.8 °C in clinic, suprapubic tenderness without costovertebral angle tenderness, and stable vital signs.

She is otherwise healthy, takes combined oral contraception, and has no history of urinary tract instrumentation or recent antibiotics. She reports a new sexual partner four days ago and uses condoms intermittently. No diabetes, not pregnant, and no known immunodeficiency.

Point-of-care urinalysis: leukocyte esterase positive, nitrite positive. Urine microscopy: pyuria 250 × 10^6/L and moderate bacteriuria. Serum white cell count 8.9 × 10^9/L and C-reactive protein 6 mg/L. A midstream urine specimen was sent for culture.

Because she had no flank pain and no clinical concern for obstruction, a renal ultrasound was performed to exclude anatomic complication; study showed normal kidney size and corticomedullary differentiation with no hydronephrosis or calculi.

Urine culture grew ≥1 × 10^8 CFU/L of lactose-fermenting, oxidase-negative, motile Gram-negative rods that were indole positive and susceptible to nitrofurantoin and trimethoprim–sulfamethoxazole; ciprofloxacin resistance was not detected.

explanation: This presentation is acute uncomplicated cystitis in a young sexually active woman. Positive nitrite and leukocyte esterase with pyuria point to a urinary tract bacterial infection originating from enteric flora. The culture characteristics—lactose-fermenting, oxidase-negative, indole-positive motile Gram-negative rod—identify Escherichia coli as the causative organism. In the absence of systemic signs or anatomic abnormality, oral therapy guided by susceptibility (for example nitrofurantoin when susceptible) is appropriate.

hints.text: A 24-year-old woman presents to the student health clinic with a 3-week history of watery, bulky, foul-smelling stools and marked postprandial bloating. She reports 6–8 bowel motions per day with greasy appearance and a 3 kg weight loss. No fever, no visible blood. On exam she is afebrile, appears mildly cachectic, and has diffuse, mild abdominal tenderness without peritonism.

Previously healthy, no chronic disease, not on immunosuppressants and HIV test performed in clinic is negative. She took a short course of over-the-counter loperamide and a 3-day antibiotic prescribed by a general practitioner without symptomatic improvement. No recent hospitalisations or antibiotic exposure prior to symptom onset.

Blood tests: haemoglobin 122 g/L, white blood cell count 6.5 × 10^9/L, CRP 2 mg/L. Stool testing: faecal leukocytes absent, Sudan stain positive for excess fat, stool antigen assay positive for a protozoan; wet-mount microscopy of fresh stool demonstrated pear-shaped, motile trophozoites with two prominent nuclei and anterior flagella.

Upper endoscopy performed because of persistent malabsorption symptoms: duodenal folds looked slightly blunted and the endoscopist obtained duodenal aspirate. Direct microscopic examination of the aspirate again showed motile, flagellated organisms attached intermittently to the mucosal surface; duodenal biopsies showed mild villous blunting and increased intraepithelial lymphocytes.

On further questioning she recalls a 10-day backpacking trip in mountain terrain six weeks before presentation during which she drank untreated spring water from streams and shared a group water filter; several people in her party developed similar greasy, foul diarrhoea about one week after the trip.

explanation: The combination of prolonged fatty, foul-smelling diarrhoea with weight loss, absent faecal leukocytes, positive faecal fat, motile binucleate trophozoites on wet mount and a history of drinking untreated freshwater strongly indicates infection with Giardia lamblia. Diagnosis is commonly confirmed by stool antigen or microscopy and may be supported by duodenal aspirate or biopsy showing organisms and villous blunting. Effective oral therapies include nitroimidazole agents or nitazoxanide; treatment usually leads to clinical and microbiological cure in immunocompetent hosts.

hints.text: A 4-year-old boy is brought to the emergency department by his parents with a 12-hour history of high fever (39.5°C), sudden onset sore throat, progressive difficulty swallowing, drooling and a muffled 'hot-potato' voice. He is sitting upright, leaning forward, and has inspiratory stridor at rest; oxygen saturation 96% on room air.

Previously healthy, developmentally normal. Family reports he fell behind on routine childhood vaccinations after moving countries and missed several infant doses of conjugate immunisations recommended against encapsulated respiratory pathogens. No recent sick contacts noted at daycare.

Blood count: white blood cells 18.2 × 10^9/L with neutrophil predominance; C-reactive protein 120 mg/L. Two sets of blood cultures were obtained. A swab of oropharyngeal secretions obtained with care for microbiology Gram stain shows small, pleomorphic Gram-negative coccobacilli among neutrophils.

Lateral neck radiograph obtained after airway precautions demonstrates a markedly enlarged, rounded and swollen epiglottic shadow producing the classic 'thumbprint' appearance; there is no cervical spine abnormality.

After emergent airway support and initiation of intravenous third-generation cephalosporin, the child defervesced and drooling decreased over 48 hours. Epiglottic swab cultured on enriched media grew small colonies that required both haemin (X factor) and NAD (V factor) for growth and were positive for a beta-lactamase test.

explanation: Presentation of acute febrile stridor, drooling and a thumbprint sign in a young child who missed routine conjugate immunisations points to invasive infection of the epiglottis. The Gram stain showing small pleomorphic Gram-negative coccobacilli plus culture growth requiring haem and NAD, together with beta-lactamase production and clinical response to a third-generation cephalosporin, identify Haemophilus influenzae as the causative organism. Lack of routine conjugate vaccination increases risk for invasive disease from encapsulated strains; beta-lactamase positivity explains potential failure of ampicillin/amoxicillin without a beta-lactamase inhibitor.

hints.text: A 45-year-old man presents to clinic with a 3-month history of gnawing epigastric pain. Pain is worst on an empty stomach and often wakes him at night; it is partially relieved by eating. He reports two episodes of dark, tarry stools over the previous week and an unintentional 3-kg weight loss. On exam he is afebrile, blood pressure 122/76 mmHg, pulse 78/min; abdominal exam shows mild epigastric tenderness without guarding or rebound.

He has no chronic illnesses, does not take aspirin or regular nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs, and has not been on anticoagulation. He tried over-the-counter antacids and took a proton-pump inhibitor intermittently for a few days with only modest relief. He works as an accountant, has no recent travel, and has not had prior upper endoscopy.

Laboratory studies show haemoglobin 100 g/L with mean corpuscular volume 78 fL, white cell count 6.8 × 10^9/L and platelets 260 × 10^9/L. Faecal occult blood test is positive. A 13C-urea breath test performed while off acid suppression is positive.

Upper gastrointestinal endoscopy demonstrates an inflamed antral mucosa and a single 1.5 cm clean-based ulcer on the anterior wall of the duodenal bulb; biopsy specimens were taken from the antrum at the time of endoscopy.

He completed a 14-day eradication regimen consisting of a proton-pump inhibitor plus two oral antibiotics. At 8-week follow-up his epigastric pain has resolved, stools are normal, and repeat 13C-urea breath testing is negative.

explanation: Clinical picture: chronic epigastric pain worse with fasting and nocturnal pain, duodenal ulcer on endoscopy, iron-deficiency anaemia with positive faecal occult blood, positive urea breath test, and symptom resolution plus negative test after targeted eradication therapy — together these findings identify infection with the gastric urease-producing bacterium responsible for most peptic ulcer disease (Helicobacter pylori). Management in this patient was appropriate: acid suppression combined with two antibiotics to eradicate the organism, followed by test-of-cure with a noninvasive urea breath test.

hints.text: A 24-year-old woman presents to the student health clinic with 48 hours of painful grouped clear vesicles on the right vermillion border extending onto the adjacent labial mucosa. She has odynophagia, malaise and a fever to 38.5 °C; examination shows tender ipsilateral submandibular lymphadenopathy and shallow ulceration where several vesicles have ruptured.

She is otherwise healthy, no chronic medications, no immunosuppression. She recalls a similar episode during her first year at university after heavy sun exposure; she reports stressful exam week beginning five days before symptoms. She denies recent dental work and reports one long-term partner without genital symptoms.

Blood count shows WBC 7.8 × 10^9/L, CRP 8 mg/L. A swab of a fresh vesicle underwent nucleic acid amplification and returned positive for a double-stranded DNA virus; a direct smear from lesion scraping demonstrated multinucleated epithelial giant cells.

Panoramic dental radiograph and point-of-care soft-tissue ultrasound of the face show no odontogenic focus or abscess; ultrasound confirms a 15 mm reactive lymph node in the right submandibular chain without drainable collection.

She was started on oral aciclovir and reported decreased pain within 24 hours; vesicles crusted by 72 hours and healed over 7–10 days. The clinical pattern, lesion location and positive lesion PCR led the team to identify the orolabial recurrent DNA virus most commonly acquired in childhood and known to respond to nucleoside-analogue therapy.

explanation: Diagnosis: recurrent orolabial infection due to Herpes simplex virus type 1. Key supportive data: painful grouped vesicles on the vermillion border with regional lymphadenopathy and fever; history of prior similar episodes triggered by sunlight/stress; Tzanck smear showing multinucleated giant cells and PCR positive for a double-stranded DNA virus; rapid improvement with aciclovir. Management for acute symptomatic episodes uses oral antivirals; counselling includes recognition of triggers and advice about transmission during active lesions.

hints.text: A 24-year-old woman presents to a sexual health clinic with a 3-day history of painful grouped vesicles on the left labia majora and minora. She reports burning pain with urination, difficulty voiding because of the pain, a subjective fever and chills, and a tender ipsilateral inguinal lymph node on examination. She denies previous similar lesions.

Sexual history notable for a single new male partner 10 days before symptom onset; intercourse was unprotected. She has no known chronic medical conditions, takes combined oral contraception, is not pregnant, and has no history of immunosuppression or prior sexually transmitted infections.

A swab from a vesicle underwent cytologic examination: multinucleated epithelial giant cells with nuclear moulding were reported. Nucleic-acid amplification from the lesion detected viral DNA consistent with an enveloped double-stranded DNA virus. Serum type-specific IgG against the two common anogenital viral serotypes was negative, suggesting a primary infection.

Point-of-care pelvic ultrasound showed no abscess or adnexal collection. Bladder scan after voiding showed a small postvoid residual volume (<50 mL), and there was no sonographic evidence of pelvic inflammatory disease.

Oral aciclovir was started within 48 hours of presentation; the patient reported marked reduction in pain within 48 hours and lesions began to crust by day 4 with re-epithelialisation by day 10. Her partner developed a similar painful genital eruption approximately two weeks after their encounter.

explanation: This presentation is most consistent with primary genital infection caused by herpes simplex virus type 2. Key supportive features: painful grouped genital vesicles with inguinal adenopathy and dysuria following recent unprotected sexual exposure; cytology showing multinucleated giant epithelial cells; lesion PCR demonstrating an enveloped double-stranded DNA virus; absence of type-specific IgG consistent with a first infection; and rapid clinical response to aciclovir with transmission to the sexual partner. Management includes antiviral therapy for symptom control and counselling about transmission and recurrence.

hints.text: A 24-year-old man presents to student health with a 3-week history of fever, sore throat, night sweats and a diffuse, nonpruritic maculopapular rash concentrated on the trunk. He reports painful posterior cervical and axillary lymph nodes and several small painful oral aphthous ulcers. He has lost 6 kg over the month and has odynophagia but no dyspnoea.

He is otherwise healthy, takes no chronic medications, and has no recent travel. He reports a new sexual partner 4 weeks before symptom onset and recent unprotected receptive anal intercourse. He denies injection drug use. He had a routine STI screen 12 months ago that was negative.

Blood tests show WBC 3.5 × 10^9/L with lymphocytes 0.6 × 10^9/L and platelets 120 × 10^9/L. ALT 85 U/L, CRP 28 mg/L. Heterophile test negative; throat swab negative for group A streptococcus. A fourth-generation antigen/antibody assay was collected and is pending.

Contrast CT of the neck and chest performed for persistent odynophagia demonstrates symmetrical, non-necrotic enlargement of multiple cervical and axillary lymph nodes (largest short axis 12 mm) and several small mediastinal nodes; no focal pulmonary consolidation or abscess.

Because clinical suspicion was high, combination antiretroviral therapy (two nucleoside analogue agents plus an integrase inhibitor) was started after laboratory confirmation. Baseline plasma viral RNA was 1.1 × 10^6 copies/mL and CD4 count was 380 × 10^6/L; at 12 weeks viral RNA fell to <50 copies/mL and CD4 rose to 610 × 10^6/L with resolution of fever and lymphadenopathy.

explanation: The presentation—acute febrile mononucleosis-like illness with mucocutaneous ulcers, generalized lymphadenopathy, recent high-risk sexual exposure, elevated transaminase and lymphopenia—followed by a very high plasma viral RNA and rapid suppression with combination antiretroviral therapy identifies primary infection with Human immunodeficiency virus 1. The diagnostic arc is supported by the clinical syndrome, imaging showing diffuse small nodes rather than focal abscess, and virologic response to antiretroviral treatment with rising CD4 count.

hints.text: A 35-year-old office worker presents in mid-winter with a 48-hour history of abrupt high fever to 39.4 °C, severe myalgias, frontal headache, sore throat and a dry cough; he reports profound malaise and was unable to attend work yesterday. On exam he is febrile, pulse 110/min, respiratory rate 18/min, oxygen saturation 97% on room air; lungs are clear to auscultation.

He has no chronic medical conditions, is not immunosuppressed and takes no regular medications. He declined this season's respiratory virus vaccination. His partner developed very similar symptoms two days before him after attending a community clinic where multiple patients had acute respiratory illness; no recent travel or animal exposures.

Blood count shows leukopenia with WBC 3.4 × 10^9/L and lymphocytes 0.9 × 10^9/L; C-reactive protein 30 mg/L. A nasopharyngeal swab was sent for multiplex molecular testing; preliminary result detected a single-stranded, negative-sense, segmented RNA respiratory virus with haemagglutinin and neuraminidase gene targets present; subtyping pending.
Chest radiograph obtained in the emergency department shows no focal lobar consolidation, only mild peribronchial cuffing; there is no pleural effusion and the cardiomediastinal silhouette is normal.

Because symptom onset was within 36 hours, treatment with a neuraminidase inhibitor (oral agent) was started. By 48 hours after first dose the patient reported marked improvement in fever, myalgias and cough; repeat nasopharyngeal molecular assay on day 3 demonstrated a large drop in viral RNA signal. Local public health laboratory later confirmed the haemagglutinin/neuraminidase pattern matched the season's circulating human respiratory virus subtype.

explanation: The clinical picture—abrupt high fever, severe myalgias and respiratory symptoms in mid-winter with household transmission—fits an acute seasonal respiratory virus. Laboratory detection of a segmented, negative-sense single-stranded RNA virus with haemagglutinin and neuraminidase gene targets plus rapid clinical and virologic response to a neuraminidase inhibitor identifies Influenza A virus as the pathogen.

hints.text: A 62-year-old man presented to the emergency department with a 24-hour history of high fever, pleuritic right-sided chest pain, and a productive cough yielding thick, blood-tinged sputum; he reported increasing breathlessness and rigors overnight.

He drinks spirits daily (≈5–6 units/day), has type 2 diabetes treated with metformin, and smokes 30 pack‑years. No recent hospitalization, no indwelling devices, and no recent antibiotics. Family reports he became acutely more lethargic the morning before admission.

On arrival: temperature 39.8°C, heart rate 112/min, blood pressure 110/68 mmHg, SpO2 88% on room air. Blood tests: WBC 18.6 × 10^9/L, CRP 210 mg/L, glucose 14.2 mmol/L. Sputum Gram stain showed many neutrophils and numerous Gram-negative rods; blood cultures drawn.

Chest radiograph demonstrated dense lobar consolidation of the right upper lobe with air bronchograms and a hemispheric bulging of the horizontal fissure; small early cavitary change at the consolidation edge.

Empiric ampicillin was started but after 24–36 hours the patient remained febrile and hypoxic. Sputum cultures subsequently grew mucoid, lactose‑fermenting colonies on selective agar that proved resistant to ampicillin but susceptible to ceftriaxone; the patient defervesced and oxygenation improved within 48 hours after switching to intravenous ceftriaxone.

explanation: The presentation of lobar right upper‑lobe pneumonia in an older man with heavy alcohol use, thick blood‑tinged (“currant‑jelly”) sputum, Gram‑negative rods on sputum stain, radiographic bulging fissure and mucoid lactose‑fermenting colonies with intrinsic ampicillin resistance that respond to a third‑generation cephalosporin is most consistent with Klebsiella pneumoniae.

hints.text: A 29-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a single, non-healing skin lesion on her left forearm. It began about 10 weeks earlier as a papule after an insect bite and gradually enlarged into a 2.2 cm shallow ulcer with a raised, indurated, erythematous border and central crust; she reports mild ipsilateral axillary tenderness but no fever or systemic symptoms. Two separate courses of oral flucloxacillin failed to improve the lesion.

She is otherwise healthy, HIV test negative six months ago, no diabetes, not on immunosuppressants. She spent 8 weeks volunteering in rural northern Peru three months before presentation; she slept in hammocks without nets and recalls frequent nocturnal bites. No memorable animal bite or freshwater exposure. No weight loss, night sweats or prolonged fever.

Dermatoscopic description from clinic photographs shows a central ulcer with overlying crust, a rolled, violaceous, indurated margin and several smaller satellite papules; soft-tissue ultrasound of the lesion demonstrates a superficial hypoechoic area confined to dermis and subcutis without collection or deeper extension.

Local community health workers in the Peruvian village had described villagers who develop similar chronic cutaneous ulcers after sleeping outdoors; she specifically recalls numerous small nocturnal flies biting exposed arms while in the hammocks. No known contacts had systemic illness.

Punch biopsy with Giemsa-stained smear and touch preps shows numerous macrophages containing 2–4 µm round bodies with an eccentric, rod-like kinetoplast; tissue PCR for kinetoplast DNA returned positive. Routine bloods: Hb 118 g/L, WBC 6.4 × 10^9/L, CRP 8 mg/L.

explanation: Chronic painless ulcer with rolled edges after nocturnal insect exposure in a rural New World setting, plus identification of small intracellular bodies with an eccentric kinetoplast on Giemsa and positive kinetoplast PCR, indicates infection by a kinetoplastid protozoan of the Leishmania genus causing cutaneous disease. Management decisions (local versus systemic therapy) depend on species, lesion size and location and risk for mucosal involvement; in New World infections evaluation for mucosal involvement and selection of systemic antiprotozoal therapy are important.

hints.text: A 32-year-old woman at 34 weeks' gestation presents to the maternity unit with 24 hours of fever (measured 38.6°C), frontal headache, photophobia, nausea and new neck stiffness. She is alert but uncomfortable; temperature 38.4°C, heart rate 110/min, blood pressure 110/70 mmHg. Obstetric assessment: fetal heart rate 150/min with preserved variability; no uterine tenderness.

G2P1, otherwise healthy, pregnancy uncomplicated except for diet-controlled gestational diabetes. She reports mild watery diarrhoea beginning two days before the fever but no recent respiratory symptoms, no immunosuppressive medications, no recent hospital admissions or invasive procedures. She works part-time in an office and has not travelled recently.

Peripheral blood: WBC 13.5 × 10^9/L with 82% neutrophils; CRP 98 mg/L. Lumbar puncture: opening pressure 22 cmH2O; CSF WBC 420 × 10^6/L with 85% neutrophils, protein 1.2 g/L, glucose 1.6 mmol/L (concurrent serum glucose 5.2 mmol/L). Gram stain of CSF shows small, weakly staining Gram‑positive coccobacillary rods; blood cultures subsequently flagged positive with organisms of similar morphology.

MRI brain with contrast demonstrates prominent leptomeningeal enhancement over the basal cisterns and posterior fossa consistent with meningitis. Obstetric ultrasound shows a single live fetus with normal biometry and Dopplers at 34 weeks.

On directed dietary questioning she recalls eating a wedge of soft unpasteurised cheese and a small amount of cold-smoked fish from a local market five days before symptoms. Public health cultured the same cheese: isolates were small beta‑haemolytic, catalase‑positive, weakly staining Gram‑positive rods that grew at 4°C and demonstrated tumbling motility; the cheese isolate matched the patient's blood isolate by basic biochemical profile.

explanation: Third‑trimester pregnancy with febrile meningitis, neutrophil‑predominant CSF, small Gram‑positive coccobacilli in CSF and blood, and epidemiologic link to unpasteurised dairy that yielded a cold‑tolerant, tumbling, catalase‑positive Gram‑positive rod identify Listeria monocytogenes. Management considerations include intravenous ampicillin (often with gentamicin) and coordination with obstetrics and public health for maternal and fetal surveillance and product recall.

hints.text: A 24-year-old woman presents to urgent care with 4 days of high fever (up to 39.6 °C), dry cough and sore throat, and 1 day of a spreading red rash. The rash began at the hairline and behind the ears and has become confluent on the face and upper chest. She reports red, gritty eyes and increased photophobia. On exam she has injected conjunctivae, tender preauricular lymphadenopathy, and a diffuse maculopapular erythematous rash; oxygen saturation 98% on room air.

She emigrated at age 16 from a country without a routine childhood vaccination programme and has no reliable record of immunisations. She works as a university outreach volunteer, living in shared housing with other students. No chronic illnesses, not pregnant, no regular medications.

Full blood count: WBC 3.4 × 10^9/L, lymphocytes 0.6 × 10^9/L, platelets 180 × 10^9/L. C-reactive protein 18 mg/L. ALT 64 U/L. Throat swab sent for viral PCR; blood sent for serology. Rapid influenza and streptococcal tests negative.

Chest radiograph shows peribronchial cuffing and patchy bilateral perihilar interstitial markings without focal lobar consolidation — appearance most consistent with viral lower respiratory tract involvement rather than lobar bacterial pneumonia.

On further questioning she reports a 2-year-old nephew who visited the week before and was unvaccinated; he had fever and a rash one week earlier and the paediatrician documented multiple small white spots on the buccal mucosa opposite the lower molars before his rash appeared.

explanation: The combination of prodromal fever with cough, coryza and conjunctivitis; a cephalocaudally spreading maculopapular rash that began at the hairline; marked lymphopenia; viral-appearing interstitial chest changes; and a clear exposure to a young unvaccinated contact with buccal enanthem is most consistent with infection by measles virus (rubeola). Diagnosis is confirmed by detection of viral RNA by PCR or by measles-specific IgM serology. Management for this patient is supportive, airborne precautions, and consideration of vitamin A supplementation given risk of respiratory complications.

hints.text: A 24-year-old male university student presents with 48 hours of progressive, painful swelling in front of his left ear and jaw. He reports low-grade fever (measured 38.2 °C), malaise and worsening pain with chewing. On examination there is a firm, tender swelling over the left parotid region extending to the angle of the mandible; intraoral inspection shows erythema at the opening of the Stensen duct without purulent discharge.

Symptoms began after a week of attending rehearsals for a student theatre production. He recalls childhood immunisations but has no documentation of a second adolescent dose; he is otherwise healthy, non-smoker, and not on regular medications. Three days after the parotid swelling began he developed acute right-sided testicular pain and swelling; he denies urethral discharge, dysuria, or recent new sexual partners.

White cell count 7.9 × 10^9/L with neutrophils 5.1 × 10^9/L. C-reactive protein 18 mg/L. Serum amylase elevated at 760 U/L (reference range 30–110 U/L). Urine pregnancy test not applicable. Urethral swab PCR for common sexually transmitted pathogens was negative.

Ultrasound of the left parotid shows diffuse gland enlargement with hypoechoic, lobular change and increased vascularity; no sialolithiasis. Scrotal ultrasound demonstrates an enlarged right testis with heterogeneous echotexture, increased intratesticular blood flow and a small reactive hydrocele, consistent with orchitis.

Several classmates who attended the same rehearsals reported similar salivary-gland swelling that week. A buccal swab from this patient tested positive by RT-PCR for the virus identified in the campus cluster; acute-phase serum contained virus-specific IgM. He was managed conservatively with analgesia, hydration and scrotal support and symptoms improved over 10 days.

explanation: The combination of acute unilateral then bilateral parotid-region swelling, elevated serum amylase, and subsequent orchitis in a postpubertal male during a campus outbreak is typical of infection with Mumps virus. Confirmation came from RT-PCR on a buccal swab and a positive virus-specific IgM; management is supportive, with attention to pain control and monitoring for complications.

hints.text: A 36-year-old man presents to the community clinic with a 3-month history of productive cough, drenching night sweats and progressive anorexia; he reports a 7-kg weight loss and two episodes of blood-streaked sputum. On examination he is thin, temperature 37.9 °C, and auscultation reveals coarse crackles over the right upper lung field.

He emigrated from Southeast Asia 5 years ago and works in a crowded bakery. He has type 2 diabetes mellitus treated with metformin (most recent HbA1c 8.1%). He received a neonatal bacillus Calmette–Guérin vaccine but has no prior history of treatment for mycobacterial disease. A rapid HIV test performed in clinic is negative.

Haemoglobin 112 g/L, white blood cell count 7.6 × 10^9/L, CRP 42 mg/L. An interferon-γ release assay returned positive. Routine sputum bacterial cultures were negative; three early-morning sputum specimens were sent for mycobacterial studies and nucleic acid amplification was requested.

Chest radiograph shows a dense cavitary opacity in the right upper zone. CT thorax demonstrates a 3.5-cm thick-walled cavitary lesion in the posterior segment of the right upper lobe with adjacent tree-in-bud centrilobular nodularity and upper-lobe predominant bronchiectatic change.

While awaiting molecular results he was started empirically on a standard four-drug regimen (isoniazid, rifampicin, pyrazinamide, ethambutol). Within two weeks his fever and night sweats resolved and cough decreased substantially. After three weeks, cultures on selective Löwenstein–Jensen medium produced slow-growing, dry rough colonies; smear from those colonies demonstrated slender acid-fast bacilli.

explanation: The combination of chronic respiratory symptoms, night sweats, weight loss, upper-lobe cavitary disease with tree-in-bud nodularity, a positive interferon-γ release assay, slow-growing rough colonies on Lowenstein–Jensen and acid-fast bacilli on smear points to infection with Mycobacterium tuberculosis. Confirmation is by nucleic acid amplification and culture with susceptibility testing; initial clinical improvement after starting the standard four-drug regimen (isoniazid, rifampicin, pyrazinamide, ethambutol) is typical once appropriate therapy begins.

hints.text: A 24-year-old female medical student presents to campus health with 10 days of progressively worsening dry cough, sore throat, intermittent low-grade fevers (maximum 38.3°C), headache and malaise. She lives in a crowded university dorm; examination shows T 38.0°C, respiratory rate 18/min, SpO2 97% on room air and scattered wheeze with faint crackles at the left base but no focal lobar signs.

Previously well, non-smoker, no chronic respiratory or cardiac disease. Five days into the illness her GP prescribed oral amoxicillin for presumed bacterial bronchitis; she reports no clinical improvement after three days on that course. No recent travel, no known immunodeficiency.

Full blood count: white blood cell count 8.2 × 10^9/L with a normal differential. C-reactive protein 18 mg/L. Cold-agglutinin test positive at 1:256; paired serology sent from day 7 and day 14 shows a rising specific IgM titre against a small, cell-wall–lacking respiratory bacterium.
Chest radiograph demonstrates patchy perihilar and lower‑zone interstitial/reticulonodular opacities, greater on the left, without dense lobar consolidation or pleural effusion.

She reports that 6 of her dormmates have had a similar prolonged dry cough over the past 2 weeks; campus health documented an outbreak of atypical pneumonias among students, and many affected peers improved rapidly after a short course of a macrolide antibiotic.

explanation: Most consistent with Mycoplasma pneumoniae causing community‑acquired 'atypical' (walking) pneumonia in a young adult from a dormitory outbreak. Key clues: subacute dry cough with pharyngitis, normal or mildly elevated inflammatory markers and WBC, positive cold agglutinins with rising specific IgM, interstitial chest X‑ray pattern, and epidemiologic clustering with macrolide responsiveness. Definitive testing can include PCR of respiratory specimens or paired serology; treatment options include macrolides or doxycycline in adults.

hints.text: A 17-year-old male presented to the emergency department with a 48-hour history of worsening bifrontal headache, high fever (measured at home as 39.6 °C), nausea with repeated vomiting, photophobia and progressive confusion. On arrival he was somnolent but arousable (GCS 10), had marked neck stiffness and a single witnessed generalized tonic–clonic seizure in triage.

Previously healthy, no chronic illnesses, no regular medications, no recent sinonasal surgery. Two days before symptom onset he spent the weekend at a shallow, warm inland lake where he had been repeatedly diving and submerging his head; he recalls inhaling lake water on one dive. He had no recent travel outside the country and no known sick contacts.

Blood: leukocytosis 15.2 × 10^9/L, CRP 132 mg/L. Lumbar puncture: opening pressure 33 cm H2O; CSF WBC 1.1 × 10^9/L with 92% neutrophils, CSF protein 2.4 g/L, CSF glucose 1.0 mmol/L (simultaneous plasma glucose 5.8 mmol/L). CSF Gram stain and routine bacterial cultures were reported as no organisms seen and no growth at 24 hours.

Non-contrast CT head showed diffuse cerebral oedema with mild effacement of the ventricles. MRI performed subsequently demonstrated meningeal enhancement and patchy T2 hyperintensity and restricted diffusion in the olfactory bulbs and medial frontal lobes bilaterally, without a discrete abscess.

Public-health investigators confirmed warm-season recreational use of the lake, surface water temperatures above 25 °C, and multiple reports that swimmers had forcefully dunked and inhaled water. The patient and witnesses emphasised forceful nasal aspiration of lake water during head-first dives in shallow areas.

explanation: Acute fulminant meningoencephalitis in a healthy adolescent with rapid onset after forceful nasal exposure to warm freshwater, neutrophilic CSF with very low glucose and negative Gram stain, and MRI involvement of the olfactory bulbs/medial frontal lobes points to primary amoebic meningoencephalitis due to Naegleria fowleri. The exposure history (warm freshwater with nasal aspiration) is the key discriminating clue; diagnosis is supported by the CSF profile and imaging pattern. Early recognition is crucial because the condition progresses rapidly and has a high mortality without urgent targeted therapy.

hints.text: A 28-year-old woman from a riverside village presents with 3 months of progressive fatigue, lightheadedness on standing and exertional dyspnoea. She has intermittent, dull lower‑abdominal discomfort but no vomiting or haematemesis. On examination she is pale with tachycardia (heart rate 110/min), normal blood pressure, soft non‑tender abdomen, and several faint, scaly, pruritic papules on the dorsum of both feet.

She works barefoot in irrigated rice paddies and collects water from the nearby river for household use. She reports no recent travel outside the province, no prior anthelmintic therapy in childhood, and a mainly rice‑based diet with limited meat or iron supplementation. No chronic illnesses or pregnancy.

Complete blood count: haemoglobin 78 g/L, mean corpuscular volume 63 fL, white blood cell count 8.6 × 10^9/L with eosinophils 1.9 × 10^9/L. Serum ferritin 8 μg/L. Faecal occult blood test positive on two separate samples.

A chest radiograph obtained because of a brief, dry cough 3 weeks earlier showed a small patchy peripheral infiltrate in the right lower zone; a film obtained during this visit shows resolution of that shadowing.

She was treated with a single oral dose of albendazole and started on oral iron. Over the next 48 hours she noted passage of several small, curved, reddish worms approximately 8–11 mm long. A post‑treatment stool specimen examined by microscopy demonstrated numerous ovoid, thin‑shelled eggs measuring about 60 × 40 μm; eosinophil count fell and symptoms and haemoglobin improved over 4 weeks.

explanation: The patient’s chronic iron‑deficiency anaemia with positive faecal occult blood, peripheral eosinophilia, history of barefoot exposure in wet soil, transient pulmonary infiltrate during a prior cough, and passage of small curved blood‑feeding nematodes with characteristic ovoid thin‑shelled eggs after treatment are consistent with infection by Necator americanus, a blood‑feeding hookworm acquired by larval skin penetration and treated with benzimidazole anthelmintics and iron repletion.

hints.text: A 24-year-old woman presents to a walk-in sexual health clinic with a 3-day history of purulent vaginal discharge, dysuria and dyspareunia. She reports mild suprapubic discomfort but no fever or rigours. On pelvic exam there is mucopurulent endocervical discharge and cervical friability; vitals are stable.

She is nulliparous, takes combined oral contraception, and is not pregnant by home test. She reports a single new male sexual partner 7 days before symptom onset; condoms were not used. She had treated chlamydial urethritis 2 years ago and has no chronic medical problems or immunosuppressive medications.

Endocervical swab Gram stain shows numerous polymorphonuclear leukocytes with intracellular Gram‑negative diplococci. Urine pregnancy test negative; full blood count WBC 7.2 × 10^9/L, C‑reactive protein 12 mg/L.

Transvaginal ultrasound performed to exclude upper genital tract involvement shows a normal-sized uterus, normal-appearing adnexa and no tubo‑ovarian abscess; only small amount of cul‑de‑sac fluid consistent with pelvic inflammation.

The patient reports that her partner developed a purulent urethral discharge around the same time; his urethral culture yielded oxidase‑positive, kidney‑bean–shaped diplococci that produced acid from glucose but not from maltose.

explanation: The presentation of acute mucopurulent cervicitis in a young sexually active patient, plus a Gram stain showing intracellular Gram‑negative diplococci and a partner whose culture grew oxidase‑positive, kidney‑bean‑shaped diplococci that ferment glucose only, identifies Neisseria gonorrhoeae. Management includes appropriate single‑dose parenteral third‑generation cephalosporin therapy per local guidelines, empiric management or testing for chlamydia, and partner notification and treatment. Culture and antimicrobial susceptibility testing are useful given rising resistance.

hints.text: A 19-year-old male university student presented to the emergency department after 12 hours of rapidly worsening symptoms: fever to 39.5°C, severe bifrontal headache, photophobia and neck stiffness. On examination he was drowsy, tachycardic at 120 min−1 and hypotensive with systolic blood pressure 90 mmHg; multiple non-blanching petechial lesions were noted on the trunk and lower limbs.

He had been well previously, no chronic medical conditions, and had not undergone splenectomy. He lives in shared university halls and attended several crowded indoor lectures this week. He reported a mild sore throat two days earlier. He had not received the relevant adolescent meningococcal vaccine series.

Blood tests: leukocyte count 16.8 × 10^9/L with neutrophil predominance and CRP 220 mg/L. Lumbar puncture: opening pressure 30 cm H2O; cerebrospinal fluid white cell count 3 200 × 10^6/L with 92% neutrophils, glucose 1.6 mmol/L (concurrent serum glucose 5.5 mmol/L), protein 2.0 g/L. CSF Gram stain demonstrated Gram‑negative diplococci within polymorphonuclear cells. Blood and CSF cultures drawn and sent.

Non-contrast CT head performed emergently showed no mass lesion or major haemorrhage and appeared appropriate for lumbar puncture. Subsequent contrast-enhanced MRI demonstrated diffuse leptomeningeal enhancement consistent with meningitis, without focal abscess or infarct.

On further questioning he said three other residents of his flat developed abrupt febrile illness this week; local public health was notified of a cluster of invasive meningitis among students and offered single-dose oral antibiotic prophylaxis to close contacts.

explanation: The combination of acute meningitis with hypotension and petechial rash, CSF showing neutrophilic pleocytosis with low glucose and Gram‑negative diplococci inside neutrophils, plus a cluster of cases among close contacts in shared accommodation, is most consistent with invasive disease caused by Neisseria meningitidis. Public-health contact prophylaxis and meningococcal immunisation of exposed cohorts are the expected responses.

hints.text: A 28-year-old man presents to the emergency department with 36 hours of crampy abdominal pain and profuse diarrhoea. He reports six to ten loose stools in the past 24 hours, some with visible blood, and subjective fevers; on arrival he is febrile to 38.9°C, tachycardic at 110/min, and appears mildly dehydrated with dry mucous membranes and orthostatic tachycardia.

Previously healthy, no chronic disease, not pregnant, no regular medications, and no recent antibiotic use. He denied recent travel outside the region. He works as a line cook and says he treated himself with over‑the‑counter antidiarrhoeal medication before coming to hospital because symptoms were disrupting a busy shift.

Blood tests: white cell count 14.8 × 10^9/L with neutrophils 12.0 × 10^9/L, C‑reactive protein 120 mg/L. Stool microscopy shows fecal leukocytes and occult blood. Preliminary stool culture: a motile, oxidase‑negative, non‑lactose‑fermenting gram‑negative bacillus grew on selective media and produced hydrogen sulfide, forming dark colonies on H2S‑detecting agar.

Contrast CT abdomen performed because of persistent severe pain: segmental wall thickening of the terminal ileum and proximal right colon with adjacent mesenteric fat stranding, consistent with acute enterocolitis rather than isolated small‑bowel obstruction.

On detailed questioning he reports preparing and eating undercooked roast chicken at a staff barbecue about 48 hours before symptom onset; two colleagues who ate the same dish developed similar diarrhoeal illness. The municipal public‑health lab reported matching hydrogen‑sulfide–producing enteric isolates from the patient's stool and a leftover chicken sample.

explanation: The clinical picture—acute febrile crampy diarrhoea with some blood, neutrophilic leukocytosis, fecal leukocytes, CT showing ileocolitis, and a point‑source exposure to undercooked poultry—together with a non‑lactose‑fermenting, motile, H2S‑producing gram‑negative bacillus recovered from stool and food, is most consistent with a non‑typhoidal Salmonella enteric infection (foodborne enterocolitis). Management for this previously healthy adult focused on fluid resuscitation and electrolyte replacement; antibiotics were withheld because the illness was self‑limited and he was not immunocompromised, in line with local practice for uncomplicated presentations.

hints.text: A 24-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with abrupt onset of profuse non‑bloody vomiting and watery diarrhoea that began 12 hours earlier while she was on a 3‑day leisure cruise. She reports severe abdominal cramping, lightheadedness when standing and has had six episodes of vomiting and four loose stools. On exam she is tachycardic to 110 bpm, blood pressure 95/60 mmHg, temperature 37.8°C, with dry mucous membranes and decreased skin turgor.

Previously healthy university student; no chronic medical conditions, no regular medications, not pregnant and no recent antibiotic use. Symptoms began abruptly after an evening buffet on the ship; over the next 24 hours several fellow passengers developed similar symptoms. She has not had bloody stools, high fever, jaundice or recent hospitalizations.

Serum tests show WBC 7.6 × 10^9/L, sodium 131 mmol/L, creatinine 140 μmol/L (baseline unknown), C‑reactive protein 6 mg/L. Stool bacterial cultures and stool microscopy for ova and parasites are negative; stool specimen shows no fecal leukocytes.

A non‑contrast abdominal radiograph obtained to exclude obstruction demonstrates non‑specific small‑bowel gas pattern without dilatation. No focal organomegaly or free air; bedside ultrasound shows small‑bowel peristalsis without localized fluid collection.

Shipboard public‑health report documents an outbreak affecting ~140 of 350 passengers and several food‑service crew with median incubation ≈ 24 hours and illness duration 48–72 hours; public‑health laboratory RT‑PCR on residual stool specimens detected a small, non‑enveloped, positive‑sense single‑stranded RNA virus consistent with the outbreak agent.

explanation: The abrupt, vomiting‑predominant gastroenteritis with short incubation after a shared buffet on a cruise, high attack rate among passengers and crew, negative bacterial studies and brief self‑limited course, together with RT‑PCR detection of a small non‑enveloped positive‑sense RNA virus in stools, identify norovirus as the most likely pathogen.

hints.text: A 19-month-old boy is brought to urgent care at 22:00 for a 24‑hour history of a loud, 'barking' cough and progressive hoarseness.
