Symptoms worsened overnight with audible inspiratory stridor and intercostal retractions; temperature recorded at home was 38.2 °C. He remains able to drink and has oxygen saturation 96% on room air. He attends a local daycare.

Term infant with normal development, up-to-date immunisations including DTaP and Hib, no chronic cardiorespiratory disease and no prior intubations. Illness began with nasal congestion and mild cough two days ago. An older sibling in the same household recently had a 'cold.' No recent travel or antibiotic use.

Blood: WBC 7.8 × 10^9/L (neutrophils 4.5, lymphocytes 2.8 × 10^9/L). CRP 12 mg/L. Rapid antigen assays for influenza A/B and respiratory syncytial virus negative. A nasopharyngeal multiplex PCR panel has been sent.
AP neck radiograph obtained because of persistent stridor shows marked subglottic narrowing with tapering of the upper trachea (steeple appearance); epiglottis appears normal.

Public-health follow-up at the daycare found five of twelve toddlers in the same room developed similar barking cough and stridor over the preceding week. Multiplex PCR performed on an affected sibling returned positive for a single non‑influenza respiratory virus that is the most common cause of acute viral laryngotracheitis in young children.

explanation: Most likely diagnosis: acute viral croup due to human parainfluenza virus. Supporting features: typical age group, nocturnal barking cough with inspiratory stridor and retractions, subglottic narrowing on neck radiograph, clustering of similar illness in daycare, and negative influenza/RSV tests with a single-virus PCR result from a contact. Management is supportive—single-dose oral dexamethasone and nebulised epinephrine for moderate to severe respiratory distress; antibiotics are not indicated unless a secondary bacterial complication is suspected.

hints.text: A 7‑year‑old boy is brought to primary care with a 3‑day history of low‑grade fever and sore throat; on day 3 his parents noted sudden bright erythema of both cheeks and a lace‑like maculopapular erythematous rash spreading over the trunk and proximal limbs. He is alert, well perfused, afebrile in clinic, without mucosal lesions or joint swelling. He attends a local kindergarten.

Born at term, no chronic medical problems, immunisations up to date, no prior transfusions and no known haemoglobinopathy. Household includes a 28‑week pregnant mother and a 2‑year‑old sibling. Parents report the child returned to school 2 days before onset; one classmate had similar facial redness the previous week.

Haemoglobin 125 g/L, leukocytes 6.8 × 10^9/L, platelets 290 × 10^9/L; peripheral smear without schistocytes. Serology on a blood sample from the child: agent‑specific IgM positive with absent IgG; PCR from a throat swab detected viral DNA consistent with acute infection.

Managed supportively with paracetamol and emollients. Fever and pharyngitis resolved within 48 hours; the facial erythema faded and the truncal reticular rash became less conspicuous over 5–7 days. No drop in haemoglobin or signs of haemolysis were observed during follow‑up.

Public‑health contact tracing identified an outbreak in the kindergarten (multiple classmates with identical cheek rash). The child’s pregnant mother was screened 1 week after exposure and had agent‑specific IgM positive and IgG negative; a follow‑up foetal ultrasound 10 days later showed new ascites and a small pericardial effusion consistent with non‑immune hydrops.

explanation: The combination of a school‑based outbreak of a bright 'cheek' erythema with a subsequent lacy truncal rash in a school‑age child, acute IgM seropositivity with detectable viral DNA, and the maternal seroconversion with foetal hydrops is characteristic of infection with Parvovirus B19. Diagnosis in uncomplicated paediatric cases is clinical, supported by IgM or PCR; treatment is supportive in immunocompetent children. Pregnant contacts require counselling and foetal surveillance because maternal infection can lead to foetal anaemia and non‑immune hydrops fetalis.

hints.text: A 24-year-old man presents to the emergency department with a 48-hour history of fever, sore throat and diffuse myalgias followed by the acute onset of right greater than left leg weakness over the past 12 hours. He cannot bear weight on the right leg; tone is flaccid on the right with absent deep tendon reflexes at the knee and ankle. Sensation to light touch and pinprick is intact. Respiratory rate is 18/min with shallow breaths but oxygen saturation 97% on room air.

He reports a 3-day prodrome of fever with sore throat and loose stools a week before presentation. He has no chronic medical problems and takes no medications. He recalls not receiving routine childhood immunisations in his home country. No history of recent insect bites, intravenous drug use, or preceding travel illness requiring hospitalization.

CSF analysis: white blood cells 150 × 10^6/L (≈90% lymphocytes), protein 0.8 g/L, glucose 3.2 mmol/L with concurrent serum glucose 5.3 mmol/L. Blood leukocyte count 7.8 × 10^9/L; C-reactive protein 18 mg/L. Bacterial cultures from blood and CSF are negative. PCR from nasopharyngeal swab and stool detected enterovirus RNA; no other viral agents identified on the panel.

MRI of the spine demonstrates focal T2-weighted hyperintensity confined to anterior horn cells of the lumbar cord (segments L1–L4), corresponding to the clinical level of motor deficit; there is no extensive transverse myelitis and brain MRI is unremarkable.

He returned two weeks ago from a 14-day volunteer teaching placement in a rural district with limited sanitation where several children recently developed febrile illnesses followed by acute flaccid weakness; local public health investigators reported clusters of acute flaccid paralysis and recovered an enterovirus from stool specimens. He had close, prolonged contact with unvaccinated children and frequently swam in a village canal that receives sewage runoff.

explanation: This presentation is most consistent with paralytic poliomyelitis caused by poliovirus. Key features: febrile prodrome followed by acute, asymmetric flaccid paralysis with preserved sensation and areflexia; CSF pleocytosis with normal glucose; MRI showing selective anterior horn cell (motor neuron) involvement; enterovirus RNA detected in stool/throat; and absence of childhood immunisation with recent travel to an area reporting clusters of acute flaccid paralysis. Management is primarily supportive (monitoring for respiratory compromise, intensive physiotherapy) and public health notification and contact tracing are essential. Definitive confirmation is by poliovirus-specific PCR and sequencing from stool or throat specimens; prevention is by appropriate immunisation.

hints.text: A 28-year-old woman presents to urgent care with 4 days of progressive right ear pain, itchiness and purulent discharge. She is a competitive swimmer; pain is worse with tragal pressure, the external auditory canal is erythematous and markedly tender with oedema, and thick greenish otorrhoea obscures the tympanic membrane. Hearing is reduced on the right.

She swims daily in a municipal pool and uses cotton buds to dry her ears after practice. No diabetes, no recent upper respiratory infection, no prior ear surgery, and she is otherwise well. There has been minimal response to over-the-counter analgesics; no systemic symptoms.

Ear swab Gram stain shows numerous Gram-negative rods. Peripheral blood count: WBC 8.6 × 10^9/L, neutrophils 5.1 × 10^9/L. Swab plated on MacConkey produced non-lactose-fermenting colonies; an oxidase test performed at bedside was positive.
Contrast CT of the temporal bones to exclude deeper spread shows soft-tissue swelling confined to the external auditory canal without cortical bone erosion or mastoid air-cell opacification.

Local public-health testing of the training pool grew the same oxidase-positive, non-lactose-fermenting Gram-negative rod recovered from her ear; colonies produced a diffusible blue–green pigment and a characteristic fruity odour on culture plates.

explanation: The presentation—acute otitis externa in a swimmer with greenish discharge—plus Gram-negative rods that are oxidase-positive, non-lactose fermenting and produce a diffusible blue–green pigment, is diagnostic of Pseudomonas aeruginosa infection. Imaging excludes invasive disease; management is canal debridement and topical anti-pseudomonal treatment (for example, topical fluoroquinolone drops) with oral agents reserved for severe or refractory cases.

hints.text: A 24-year-old man presented to the emergency department with a 72-hour history of fever, progressive agitation, episodes of confusion, and increasing difficulty swallowing liquids; staff noted hypersalivation and intermittent gasping vocalizations. On exam he was febrile to 38.6 °C, tachycardic, restless, and had painful paresthesia localized to the right forearm; a healed puncture wound with a faint scar was present there.

He is previously healthy, not on regular medications, and reports no routine travel outside his region. He has no record of pre-exposure vaccination against the suspected pathogen and denies receiving any post-exposure injections after the forearm injury. Symptoms began approximately two weeks after the wound occurred.

Serum white blood cell count 11.5 × 10^9/L with neutrophil predominance. Cerebrospinal fluid obtained on day 3 of admission: WBC 120 × 10^6/L (≈95% lymphocytes), protein 1.1 g/L, glucose 3.6 mmol/L; Gram stain negative. Saliva RT-PCR returned positive for viral RNA; immunofluorescence of a nuchal skin biopsy demonstrated viral antigen in cutaneous peripheral nerves.

MRI brain with FLAIR sequences showed increased signal intensity in the hippocampi and dorsal brainstem without focal mass lesion or evidence of abscess; no restricted diffusion to suggest acute infarct. Findings were reported as consistent with a viral encephalitic process involving limbic structures and brainstem.

Detailed questioning revealed the forearm wound was from a bite by a stray dog in a rural region of South–East Asia 16 days before symptom onset; the dog appeared ill and died several days later. The patient did not seek medical care after the bite and did not receive any vaccine or immunoglobulin.

explanation: The combination of a short incubation after a mammalian bite, prodrome of fever and agitation progressing to encephalopathy with hydrophobia/aphagia-like features, saliva RT-PCR positive for viral RNA, antigen in cutaneous nerves, and MRI limbic/brainstem involvement is diagnostic of infection with the rabies virus. Management considerations include intensive supportive care and public health notification; prevention hinges on timely post-exposure prophylaxis (wound care, passive immunoglobulin, and active vaccination) after high-risk exposures.

hints.text: A 24-year-old primary-school teacher presents to occupational health with 48 hours of nasal congestion, profuse clear rhinorrhoea, frequent sneezing, sore throat and a dry cough. She feels mildly unwell but is ambulatory and afebrile at home. In clinic: T 37.9 °C, HR 90/min, RR 14/min, SpO2 98% on room air. Nasal mucosa is edematous with clear discharge; posterior pharynx mildly erythematous; chest auscultation clear without crackles or wheeze.

She is otherwise healthy, takes no regular medications, has intermittent allergic rhinitis and received this season’s influenza vaccine. Symptoms began after a full day teaching a class in which several pupils had runny noses; two children were sent home that morning. No recent travel, no known immunodeficiency, no animal exposures.

Basic bloodwork: white blood cell count 6.1 × 10^9/L, neutrophils 3.6 × 10^9/L, lymphocytes 2.2 × 10^9/L. C-reactive protein 4 mg/L. Point-of-care throat swab negative for group A streptococcus; rapid influenza antigen negative.
Because cough persisted despite clear chest auscultation, a chest radiograph was obtained and shows no focal consolidation, no effusion and normal cardiac silhouette.

Public-health follow-up: one symptomatic pupil had a primary-care multiplex respiratory PCR that detected a small, positive-sense single-stranded RNA virus without an envelope; the teacher’s nasopharyngeal multiplex PCR returned positive for the same viral type while assays for influenza A/B, RSV and endemic coronaviruses were negative.

explanation: Presentation and exam are consistent with an uncomplicated upper respiratory infection (common cold) in an otherwise healthy adult with recent exposure to children. Modest inflammatory markers and a normal chest radiograph argue against bacterial pneumonia. Multiplex PCR identified a non-enveloped, positive-sense single-stranded RNA virus commonly recovered from outpatient upper respiratory infections; combined with the clinical syndrome and exposure history, the most likely pathogen is rhinovirus. Management is supportive (analgesics, nasal saline, fluids, rest) and avoidance of unnecessary antibiotics; hand hygiene and workplace exclusion while febrile or symptomatic can limit spread.

hints.text: A 34-year-old man presents to the emergency department in late summer with 4 days of high fever (maximum 39.5°C), severe frontal headache, diffuse myalgias and increasing malaise. He developed a rash on day 3 that began on the wrists and ankles and has spread centrally; on examination he is febrile at 39.2°C, heart rate 110/min, blood pressure 100/60 mmHg, and appears mildly confused. The skin shows a diffuse maculopapular eruption with early petechial change on the lower legs.

Previously healthy, takes no regular medications, no immunosuppression, and no recent international travel. He first saw his primary clinician two days earlier and was given a short course of oral amoxicillin for presumed cellulitis without improvement. Symptoms progressed rapidly over 48 hours with worsening headache and new anorexia.

Laboratory testing: white blood cell count 4.8 × 10^9/L, platelet count 78 × 10^9/L, sodium 131 mmol/L, alanine aminotransferase 165 U/L, aspartate aminotransferase 140 U/L, C-reactive protein 120 mg/L. Blood cultures pending; peripheral smear shows occasional schistocytes but no parasites. Routine bacterial cultures remain negative at 24 hours.

Clinical photograph of the hands (available in the chart) demonstrates multiple petechial macules on the palmar surfaces and lateral fingers in addition to the maculopapular lesions on the forearms; chest radiograph is unremarkable.

On further questioning the patient reports a weekend camping trip in a wooded area of rural North Carolina one week earlier and recalls finding a tick attached to his right ankle for about 24–36 hours before he removed it by hand approximately 6 days before symptom onset.

explanation: The combination of high fever, severe headache, rash that began at the wrists and ankles and progressed centrally with later palmar petechiae, thrombocytopenia, hyponatraemia and transaminase elevation in a patient with a recent tick bite in an endemic area indicates infection with Rickettsia rickettsii (Rocky Mountain spotted fever). Empiric treatment with doxycycline should be started promptly; confirmatory testing can include PCR of acute blood samples or paired serology demonstrating seroconversion.

hints.text: An 8-month-old boy is brought to urgent care from his daycare centre with a 24-hour history of profuse watery diarrhoea (≈10 episodes), repeated non-bilious vomiting, and low-grade fever. Parents report markedly fewer wet nappies and a lethargic appearance. On exam he is tachycardic, has dry mucous membranes, a sunken anterior fontanelle and delayed capillary refill consistent with moderate dehydration.

Born at term, no chronic illnesses, exclusively breastfed until 6 months. Family declined the first dose of the recommended oral enteric vaccine in infancy. He attends a mixed-age daycare; no recent antibiotic exposure. No bloody stools, no travel, no recent ingestion of untreated water.

Venous chemistry: Na 132 mmol/L, K 4.2 mmol/L, HCO3− 15 mmol/L, urea 10.2 mmol/L, creatinine 30 μmol/L. CBC: WBC 7.5 × 10^9/L. Stool microscopy shows no fecal leukocytes; routine bacterial culture is pending and later returns negative.

Abdominal ultrasound performed to evaluate for complications shows diffuse small-bowel wall thickening and increased luminal gas consistent with enteritis; no intussusception, no free fluid, and no focal mass.

Local public-health follow-up reveals a wintertime outbreak at the child’s daycare affecting several infants aged 5–12 months. Rapid stool antigen testing in multiple affected infants detects a non-enveloped, segmented double-stranded RNA viral antigen. The patient improves over 48 hours with oral rehydration and brief nasogastric fluids.

explanation: An infant with acute onset of severe watery diarrhoea and vomiting, signs of dehydration, attendance at a daycare outbreak in winter, negative bacterial studies, and a positive stool antigen for a non-enveloped segmented double-stranded RNA virus is most consistent with rotavirus infection. Incomplete or absent vaccination increases risk; management centres on rehydration and supportive care.

hints.text: A 24-year-old woman presents to the outpatient clinic with 48 hours of low‑grade fever, sore throat and a spreading pink maculopapular rash. The rash began on the face and behind the ears and is now on the trunk and proximal limbs. She complains of mild joint stiffness of the wrists and has tender, enlarged posterior auricular and suboccipital lymph nodes on exam; vital signs are otherwise stable.

She is 8 weeks' pregnant, employed at a neighbourhood daycare, and reports she did not receive scheduled childhood immunisations because of irregular follow‑up. No chronic medical problems and no immunosuppressive medications. She recalls a brief febrile illness last week in one of the kindergarten classes where she works.

Throat swab PCR returned positive for the same suspected viral agent. Maternal serology shows the presence of virus‑specific IgM with an initially negative IgG; repeat serology in 10 days demonstrates rising IgG consistent with recent primary infection.

Targeted fetal ultrasound and fetal echocardiography at 13 weeks demonstrate symmetric intrauterine growth restriction and a large patent ductus arteriosus; no gross central nervous system structural abnormality is seen at this gestation.

Public-health follow-up at the daycare identified a 4‑year‑old unimmunised child who developed fever, postauricular lymphadenopathy and a facial‑onset maculopapular rash the week before the patient's symptoms; that child's acute serum showed virus‑specific IgM positivity.

explanation: Presentation of a young, unvaccinated pregnant woman with postauricular lymphadenopathy, facial‑onset maculopapular rash, arthralgia and serologic evidence of recent primary infection, combined with a fetal echocardiographic finding of patent ductus arteriosus and a linked daycare case, is diagnostic of rubella virus infection. Early maternal infection carries high risk of congenital cardiac and ocular defects; confirmation was by maternal PCR and seroconversion. Prevention is by preconception vaccination of susceptible individuals.

hints.text: A 24-year-old woman is admitted after 8 days of escalating fever (highest measured 39.5 °C), frontal headache, increasing malaise and diffuse abdominal pain. She reports constipation rather than diarrhoea. For 48 hours she has noticed a few faint, slightly raised pink macules on her anterior trunk. On examination temperature 39.2 °C, pulse 78 bpm (noted to be relatively slow for the degree of fever), abdomen soft with mild tenderness in the right lower quadrant, no rebound or guarding.

She returned 10 days ago from a 3-week visit to relatives in a provincial city in northern India. She is otherwise healthy, not pregnant, takes no regular medications, and did not receive any pre-travel vaccinations. She denies prior antibiotic treatment during or after the trip. Two household contacts abroad had brief gastroenteritis while she was there.

Contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen and pelvis performed on day 2 of admission shows segmental circumferential thickening of the terminal ileum with several enlarged adjacent mesenteric lymph nodes; no free intraperitoneal air, no focal abscess, and no bowel perforation.

While abroad she frequently ate food from street vendors and drank tap or well water; she recalls a large family meal where several guests later developed febrile illness. She did not drink bottled water consistently and consumed salads and crushed-ice desserts sold locally.

Admission bloods: WBC 3.8 × 10^9/L with relative lymphopenia, haemoglobin 116 g/L, platelets 120 × 10^9/L, CRP 96 mg/L, ALT 72 U/L. Blood cultures drawn on admission became positive at ~48 hours for a motile, oxidase-negative, non–lactose-fermenting Gram-negative bacillus; biochemical testing demonstrated hydrogen sulfide production on triple-sugar-iron agar. The isolate was susceptible to ceftriaxone and azithromycin.

explanation: The patient’s prolonged stepwise fever, relative bradycardia, faint macular trunk lesions, ileocecal inflammation on imaging, recent travel to an endemic area with consumption of untreated food and water, and blood cultures yielding a motile, non–lactose-fermenting Gram-negative bacillus that produces hydrogen sulfide are most consistent with Salmonella enterica serovar Typhi (Salmonella Typhi) bacteremia causing enteric fever. Treatment options for susceptible isolates include third-generation cephalosporins (for example ceftriaxone) or azithromycin; vaccination before travel and safe food/water practices reduce risk.

hints.text: A 48-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with 5 days of dry cough, myalgias and progressive dyspnoea; she reports sudden loss of smell on day 3 of illness. In the preceding 24 hours she developed fever and increasing breathlessness. On arrival: T 38.2 °C, pulse 98/min, RR 22/min, SpO2 91% on room air; BMI 34 kg/m2. Lung auscultation reveals fine bibasilar crackles.

Medical history notable for type 2 diabetes treated with metformin and hypertension controlled with an ACE inhibitor. She is a non-smoker, works part time as a retail cashier, and has not recently travelled. She received this season's influenza vaccine but reports she has not had any doses of the pandemic-era respiratory virus vaccine series.

Blood tests: leucocytes 4.8 × 10^9/L, lymphocytes 0.7 × 10^9/L, haemoglobin 136 g/L, platelets 210 × 10^9/L. CRP 85 mg/L, ALT 68 U/L, creatinine 82 µmol/L. Arterial blood gas on room air: PaO2 8.9 kPa (approx. SpO2 91%).

Chest radiograph and portable CT show bilateral, predominantly peripheral and lower-lobe ground-glass opacities with relative sparing of the central perihilar regions; no focal lobar consolidation or pleural effusion.

Hospital occupational health reports a cluster of respiratory illness among local retail and care workers; her nasopharyngeal reverse-transcription PCR returned positive for the same novel respiratory virus that initiated the global pandemic declared in early 2020.

explanation: The combination of several days of dry cough, acute anosmia, hypoxaemia, lymphopenia, elevated CRP and peripheral ground-glass opacities on imaging in the setting of a positive nasopharyngeal RT‑PCR for the novel pandemic virus strongly indicates infection with SARS‑CoV‑2. Risk factors (obesity, diabetes) and the described workplace/household cluster further support this diagnosis.

hints.text: A 24-year-old man presents to the clinic with three months of intermittent visible haematuria and terminal dysuria. He notes blood at the end of the urinary stream and occasional suprapubic discomfort but no fever. He has lost 3 kg over this period. On examination he is afebrile, blood pressure 118/74 mmHg, shows mild conjunctival pallor and suprapubic tenderness without peritonism.

Symptoms began insidiously; he received two short courses of oral antibiotics for presumed cystitis with only transient improvement. He has no history of urological instrumentation, no diabetes, is HIV-negative on recent testing, does not smoke, and reports no significant sexual risk exposures. No family history of haematuria or early malignancy.

Full blood count: haemoglobin 112 g/L, white cell count 9.6 × 10^9/L with eosinophils 1.6 × 10^9/L. Serum creatinine 88 μmol/L. Urinalysis: gross haematuria, microscopy with numerous erythrocytes and 10–20 leukocytes per high-power field; urine culture sterile after 48 hours; urine cytology negative for malignant cells.

Renal and pelvic ultrasound shows irregular nodular thickening of the posterior bladder wall with multiple punctate linear calcifications along the bladder mucosa and early left-sided hydroureteronephrosis. No discrete intraluminal bladder mass identified on ultrasound.

On further questioning he describes working as a river guide in sub-Saharan Africa seven months ago; he frequently waded and swam in shallow freshwater lagoons and noted an itchy papular rash on his legs on return. Haematuria began roughly three months after that travel.

explanation: The combination of persistent terminal haematuria, peripheral eosinophilia, sterile pyuria, bladder-wall calcifications with hydroureteronephrosis, and a history of freshwater exposure in sub-Saharan Africa indicates urinary schistosomiasis due to Schistosoma haematobium. Diagnosis is confirmed by finding characteristic eggs in urine; praziquantel is the recommended treatment. Complications to consider include obstructive uropathy and increased long-term risk of squamous cell carcinoma of the bladder.

hints.text: A 29-year-old woman presents to the outpatient clinic with 3 months of intermittent crampy lower abdominal pain and passages of fresh blood with stool. She reports progressive abdominal fullness and early satiety; on examination the liver edge is palpable 4 cm below the right costal margin and the spleen is palpable 6 cm below the left costal margin. Vital signs are normal and there is no stigmata of chronic alcohol use.

She returned 5 months ago from a 10-month humanitarian posting in a rural region of northeastern Brazil. While there she bathed and washed clothes in slow-moving irrigation ditches and drank treated bottled water. She recalls a brief febrile illness with urticaria and dry cough about 4 months ago that resolved without antibiotics. No prior liver disease, no heavy alcohol use, and no recent antibiotic exposure.

Full blood count: haemoglobin 95 g/L, white blood cells 11.8 × 10^9/L with eosinophils 2.6 × 10^9/L, platelets 92 × 10^9/L. Liver tests: ALT 48 IU/L, AST 54 IU/L, albumin 37 g/L. C-reactive protein 12 mg/L. Serum hepatitis B and C screens negative.

Abdominal ultrasound shows moderate splenomegaly and echogenic thickening of the periportal tracts with blunted portal branches; there is mild portal vein enlargement and collateral venous flow consistent with presinusoidal portal hypertension. No focal hepatic lesions; ascites minimal.

A stool concentration examination performed at the regional laboratory demonstrates numerous ovoid eggs approximately 120–140 µm in length; each egg has a conspicuous lateral spine on visual inspection. The patient reports the laboratory tech described the eggs as those of a trematode encountered in freshwater exposures.

explanation: Chronic intestinal and hepatosplenic disease following freshwater contact in endemic South America, peripheral eosinophilia, presinusoidal portal hypertension with periportal fibrosis, and stool microscopy showing ovoid eggs with a prominent lateral spine are diagnostic of infection with Schistosoma mansoni. The brief febrile/urticarial illness several weeks after exposure fits the acute immunologic phase, with later chronic granulomatous inflammation producing periportal fibrosis and portal hypertension.

hints.text: A 24-year-old woman presents to urgent care with 48 hours of crampy lower abdominal pain, high fever and tenesmus; she reports 8–12 small-volume bowel movements in the past 24 hours with visible blood and mucus, mild dizziness when standing, T 39.2°C and focal left lower‑quadrant tenderness on examination.

Previously healthy, no chronic medications, no recent travel or antibiotic use; she works as an assistant in a community daycare and notes several toddlers at her centre developed diarrhoea over the past week; she changed diapers for one child two days before her symptoms began.

Blood tests: leukocyte count 13.4 × 10^9/L, C‑reactive protein 120 mg/L, Na+ 133 mmol/L; stool microscopy shows numerous polymorphonuclear leukocytes and red cells; stool culture yields pale colonies on MacConkey agar, Gram stain with small Gram‑negative rods, oxidase negative and non‑motile on motility testing at 37°C.

Contrast CT abdomen performed because of persistent pain shows continuous mucosal thickening and submucosal edema of the rectosigmoid colon extending into the descending colon, without perforation or abscess—report consistent with acute colitis.

Local public‑health investigation identifies clustered cases among daycare attendees and staff; stool isolates from two infants and the patient have an identical biochemical profile (non‑lactose‑fermenting, oxidase negative, non‑motile Gram‑negative bacillus) and matching antibiogram, and caregivers reported inconsistent hand hygiene during diaper changes.

explanation: The presentation of small‑volume bloody diarrhoea with tenesmus, stool neutrophils, CT evidence of left‑sided colitis and an outbreak in a daycare with isolates that are non‑lactose‑fermenting, oxidase negative and non‑motile is most consistent with infection due to Shigella species. Transmission in childcare settings via fecal–oral spread and a low infectious dose explain the cluster. Management includes fluid and electrolyte replacement, infection control measures, and targeted antibiotic therapy guided by susceptibility (commonly oral ciprofloxacin or azithromycin depending on local resistance patterns and patient factors).

hints.text: A 30-year-old male competitive rower presents to urgent care with 48 hours of progressive pain, swelling and fever after shaving his right forearm in the team locker room. He has a painful, erythematous, fluctuant 3-cm nodule with a central area of necrosis; temperature 38.6°C and pulse 110/min.

He is otherwise well, with a history of atopic dermatitis treated intermittently with topical corticosteroid. He does not have diabetes, denies recent antibiotics, and reports sharing razors with teammates. He recalls a small 'boil' on his thigh last summer that resolved without medical care.

Peripheral blood: WBC 14.8 × 10^9/L with neutrophils 12.0 × 10^9/L; CRP 112 mg/L. Needle aspiration of the lesion yielded purulent fluid. Gram stain shows abundant neutrophils and Gram-positive cocci in clusters. Blood cultures have been drawn and are pending.

Point-of-care ultrasound of the forearm demonstrates a 3.0 × 1.8 cm well-defined, superficial hypoechoic fluid collection with posterior acoustic enhancement, located above the deep fascia and without extension into muscle or joint spaces.

Incision and drainage performed; after 24 hours the wound culture grew golden, β-hemolytic colonies on blood agar. The isolate was catalase positive and coagulase positive. A nasal swab from the patient grew an identical organism.

explanation: Purulent skin abscess after shaving, Gram-positive cocci in clusters on Gram stain, and an isolate that is catalase- and coagulase-positive with golden, β-hemolytic colonies and concordant nasal carriage identify Staphylococcus aureus as the cause of this soft-tissue infection.

hints.text: A 68-year-old woman presents to clinic with a 5-week history of progressive right knee pain, swelling, and decreased range of motion. She reports low-grade fevers and difficulty weight-bearing; symptoms began about 12 weeks after an elective total knee arthroplasty and she notes a small area of persistent scabbing near the surgical incision.

Medical history notable for type 2 diabetes treated with metformin (recent HbA1c 7.0%), hypertension, and obesity (BMI 31 kg/m^2). The immediate postoperative course was uncomplicated aside from a stitch-line seroma at 2 weeks that was opened and dressed in clinic; no prolonged hospital stay, no recent urinary catheterisation or central venous access.

Blood tests: WBC 11.2 × 10^9/L, CRP 85 mg/L, ESR 65 mm/h. Joint aspiration yielded turbid fluid with WBC 75 × 10^9/L and 87% neutrophils; Gram stain showed Gram-positive cocci in clusters. After 48 hours three aerobic cultures from the aspirate grew small, white, non-haemolytic colonies that were catalase-positive and coagulase-negative.

Plain radiographs of the right knee show a focal radiolucent line around the tibial component with mild subsidence compared with immediate post-op films; bedside ultrasound confirms a large peri-prosthetic effusion without adjacent gas or extensive osteomyelitic change.

The patient underwent prosthesis removal and thorough debridement; intraoperative tissue and implant sonicate cultures yielded the same coagulase-negative staphylococcal colonies in more than three samples that proved novobiocin-sensitive. After implant removal plus a 6-week course of targeted antimicrobials her pain, effusion and inflammatory markers resolved.

explanation: Device-associated chronic prosthetic joint infection with repeated growth of a coagulase-negative, novobiocin-sensitive staphylococcus most strongly indicates Staphylococcus epidermidis. The time course (weeks to months after arthroplasty), multiple positive cultures from prosthetic material, and clinical response only after implant removal are typical of this pathogen on prosthetic surfaces.

hints.text: A 24-year-old female university student presents to the clinic with 48 hours of dysuria, urinary frequency and suprapubic discomfort. Symptoms began abruptly the day after a weekend trip. She is afebrile, pulse 78/min, blood pressure 112/72 mmHg. Abdominal examination shows mild suprapubic tenderness without guarding; external genital and speculum examinations are unremarkable.

She reports a new sexual partner one week ago and began using spermicide-containing condoms two months earlier. No prior urinary tract infections, no diabetes, not pregnant, no recent antibiotics or instrumentation.

Urine dipstick: leucocyte esterase positive, nitrite negative. Microscopy: pyuria. Midstream urine culture grew a pure isolate at >10^5 CFU/mL. Gram stain showed Gram-positive cocci in clusters; the isolate was catalase positive and produced no clumping factor.

Given uncomplicated features, renal tract imaging was done only to exclude alternative pathology after persistent symptoms: bedside ultrasound of kidneys and bladder demonstrated normal renal cortical echotexture, no hydronephrosis and a non-distended bladder after voiding.

Empirical oral nitrofurantoin was started; her dysuria and frequency improved markedly within 48 hours. Final susceptibilities reported the urinary isolate was susceptible to nitrofurantoin but resistant to novobiocin.

explanation: Young, sexually active woman with acute, uncomplicated cystitis and a pure culture of catalase-positive, coagulase-negative Gram-positive cocci that is novobiocin resistant and nitrite-negative is most consistent with Staphylococcus saprophyticus. Postcoital exposure and response to nitrofurantoin support the diagnosis of uncomplicated lower urinary tract infection due to this pathogen.

hints.text: A 24-year-old kindergarten teacher presents with 48 hours of progressive sore throat and fever. She reports difficulty swallowing and speaking because of throat pain; temperature 39.2°C, pulse 102/min. Examination shows bilateral tonsillar erythema with white exudates, a 2-cm tender anterior cervical lymph node on the left, and no cough or nasal discharge.

Previously healthy, no regular medications, no recent travel. Symptoms began abruptly the day after supervising a classroom party. She has not taken antibiotics for this illness and notes that her symptoms have worsened rapidly over 48 hours despite analgesics.

Throat swab Gram stain demonstrated numerous Gram-positive cocci arranged in chains. Full blood count: WBC 14.2 × 10^9/L with neutrophils 11.3 × 10^9/L. C-reactive protein 78 mg/L. A clinic rapid antigen test for bacterial pharyngitis was positive.

Point-of-care ultrasound of the neck showed enlarged, asymmetrically swollen tonsils with surrounding soft-tissue oedema but no fluid collection to suggest a drainable peritonsillar abscess or deep neck-space involvement.

The school notified her that three preschool children in her class had abrupt fever and sore throat 2–3 days earlier; two developed a diffuse, fine, erythematous 'sandpaper' rash and an erythematous 'strawberry' tongue, and one child had been started on oral penicillin by the family physician.

explanation: Presentation (fever, abrupt sore throat, tonsillar exudates, tender anterior cervical lymphadenopathy, absence of cough), Gram-positive cocci in chains on throat specimen, positive rapid antigen for bacterial pharyngitis, and recent exposure to children with a sandpaper rash and strawberry tongue point to Streptococcus pyogenes infection (group A beta-hemolytic streptococcus) causing acute pharyngitis/scarletiform illness. First-line therapy is an oral penicillin or amoxicillin to reduce suppurative complications and prevent transmission.

hints.text: A 34-year-old man presents to outpatient clinic with 6 months of intermittent crampy abdominal pain and watery diarrhoea (up to 6 stools/day), progressive 6 kg weight loss, and an intensely pruritic linear rash on the buttocks that appears several times per week and seems to migrate over hours. He is afebrile; examination shows thin body habitus and erythematous, raised serpiginous tracks on the posterior thighs and buttocks with mild diffuse abdominal tenderness.

Previously healthy; no diabetes or chronic lung disease. He is not taking corticosteroids or other immunosuppressants and tested HIV-negative 8 months ago. Symptoms began insidiously after he returned from seasonal work overseas and have waxed and waned. Primary care had prescribed a 3-day course of albendazole and a short course of oral antihistamines with minimal improvement in symptoms.

Complete blood count: WBC 13.2 × 10^9/L with eosinophils 3.0 × 10^9/L (23%); haemoglobin 125 g/L. Serum total IgE markedly elevated at 1 100 kU/L. Direct stool microscopy from a fresh sample demonstrated motile rhabditiform larvae identified by the parasitology laboratory.

Chest radiograph obtained for intermittent cough shows patchy bilateral peripheral air‑space opacities that were not present on an imaging study from 12 months earlier; the pattern is compatible with transient eosinophilic pneumonitis and resolved on a radiograph 2 months later.

He worked barefoot in flooded rice fields and on small farms in rural Southeast Asia for 9 months two years before presentation, reporting frequent direct skin contact with wet soil; other household members who wore shoes regularly are asymptomatic.

explanation: The combination of chronic intermittent diarrhoea and weight loss, an intensely pruritic rapidly migrating serpiginous rash on the buttocks, marked peripheral eosinophilia with elevated IgE, transient pulmonary infiltrates, a history of prolonged barefoot contact with wet soil in an endemic region, and demonstration of motile rhabditiform larvae in stool is diagnostic of Strongyloides stercoralis. Recommended management is oral ivermectin with follow-up stool testing and serology to document eradication.

hints.text: A 24-year-old woman presents to primary care with a 3-week history of intermittent crampy abdominal pain and new perianal itching. Over the past week she and her partner have noticed small, flat, white, moving segments in formed stools; no fever, vomiting, or significant weight loss. On examination the abdomen is soft with mild diffuse tenderness; perianal inspection shows a few motile, rice-grain–like fragments.

She emigrated from a rural Andean community 8 months ago and returned there for a family visit 2 months prior. At that visit she ate several home-prepared dishes, including pork that was only lightly cooked. No chronic medical problems, HIV test last year was negative, and she takes no regular medications. Several relatives reported similar stool findings during the same visit.

Full blood count: WBC 7.8 × 10^9/L, eosinophils 0.9 × 10^9/L. A stool sample submitted for microscopy showed numerous oval eggs with a thick, radially striated shell. A passed macroscopic fragment examined by parasitology was a flattened ribbon of repeated segments (proglottids) ≈1 cm wide with a central longitudinal line.

Point-of-care abdominal ultrasound performed while the patient reported recent passage showed a long, linear, mobile echogenic structure with periodic segmentation within the ascending colon; there was no bowel wall thickening or evidence of obstruction.

Further characterization of the entire expelled specimen after clinic retrieval revealed an intact scolex with an apical rostellum bearing hooklets on microscopy; the patient confirmed repeated consumption of undercooked pork from household pigs during the family visit and relatives had submitted identical specimens to a local clinic.

explanation: This presentation and workup indicate intestinal infection by the pork tapeworm, Taenia solium. Key clues: recent ingestion of undercooked pork, visible motile proglottids in stool, ova with a radially striated shell on stool microscopy, and demonstration of a scolex with hooklets (which distinguishes the pork tapeworm from the beef tapeworm). Management is oral antiparasitic therapy (e.g., praziquantel or niclosamide per local protocol) with attention to proper disposal of the specimen and counselling about prevention (thorough cooking of pork and hygiene). Consider screening household contacts for infection.

hints.text: A 28-year-old man presents to a sexual health clinic with a single painless ulcer on the shaft of the penis that he first noticed 3 weeks ago. The lesion measures about 1.5 cm, has a clean base and indurated margins. He also has two firm, non-tender left inguinal lymph nodes. He reports no fever, urethral discharge, or dysuria.

He reports a new sexual partner 5 weeks before presentation and says intercourse was condomless. He had topical antibacterial ointment applied by a primary-care clinician without improvement. Past medical history is notable only for treated chlamydia 2 years ago; an HIV test 6 months ago was negative. He is not on any regular medications and has no recent travel or intravenous drug use.

Non-specific serologic screening returned strongly positive at a titre of 1:128. A contemporary fourth-generation HIV antigen/antibody test is negative. Full blood count is within reference range (WBC 6.8 × 10^9/L) and CRP is 8 mg/L. Dark-field microscopy of exudate from the ulcer demonstrates slender, motile, helically coiled organisms.

Clinical photographs obtained during the clinic visit 3 days later show a new, symmetric maculopapular eruption involving the trunk and both palms; lesions are non-pruritic and discrete. No mucosal ulcerations are present on oral exam.

On further questioning the patient says his recent partner had a painless genital lesion several weeks earlier and was told by their general practitioner that serology was positive; the partner reported receiving a single intramuscular injection of long-acting penicillin.

explanation: This presentation—the painless indurated genital ulcer with regional non-tender adenopathy, positive non-specific serologic test at high titre, demonstration of slender motile helical organisms on dark-field microscopy, a subsequent maculopapular rash including the palms, and a sexual partner with positive serology and intramuscular penicillin treatment—indicates infection by Treponema pallidum. First-line therapy for early infection in an immunocompetent patient remains a single intramuscular dose of benzathine penicillin; serologic titres are used for baseline and follow-up monitoring.

hints.text: A 28-year-old woman presents to urgent care with a 10-day history of fever, progressive diffuse myalgias and difficulty rising from a chair. She recalls transient watery diarrhoea for 48 hours about one week before the muscle pain began. On examination T 38.3°C, periorbital puffiness, tenderness of proximal lower-extremity muscles and hip flexor strength 4/5 bilaterally; no rash.

Previously healthy, no regular medications, not pregnant. Symptoms began approximately 7–10 days after a weekend away with several family meals; she has not had recent vaccinations, blood transfusions or hospital admissions. Weakness and myalgias have progressed despite paracetamol and ibuprofen; she reports difficulty chewing meat at dinner due to jaw discomfort starting three days ago.

Full blood count: WBC 15.0 × 10^9/L with eosinophils 3.2 × 10^9/L. CRP 48 mg/L. Serum creatine kinase 1 350 U/L (reference <200 U/L). ALT 62 U/L. Urinalysis normal. Peripheral blood film confirms eosinophilia without abnormal cells.

MRI of the thighs with STIR sequences shows symmetric oedematous T2/STIR hyperintensity of the proximal anterior and medial thigh muscles with mild enlargement but no abscess or focal fluid collection; pattern described as inflammatory myositis affecting predominantly proximal muscle groups.

On further questioning she reports eating several helpings of homemade raw-cured sausages and undercooked pork and wild boar meat at a rural family celebration three weeks before presentation; several other attendees had transient gastrointestinal upset but did not develop myalgias.

explanation: The combination of a brief prodromal diarrhoeal illness followed 1–3 weeks later by fever, periorbital oedema, prominent eosinophilia and a myositis with elevated CK after consumption of undercooked pork/wild game is most consistent with infection by Trichinella spiralis. Diagnostic confirmation is usually by serology (antibody testing) and, when needed, muscle biopsy demonstrating encysted larvae. Treatment is with an anthelmintic (albendazole or mebendazole) often combined with short-course corticosteroids for severe myositis or systemic symptoms.

hints.text: A 24-year-old woman presents to sexual health clinic with 6 days of frothy, yellow-green vaginal discharge, vulvar itching, and discomfort with intercourse. She denies fever or heavy pelvic pain. On speculum exam there is copious frothy discharge and multiple pinpoint punctate erythematous lesions on the ectocervix; uterine motion is non-tender.

She reports a new male sexual partner 3 weeks earlier and inconsistent condom use. She has had two prior episodes of vulvovaginal candidiasis diagnosed clinically last year, no recent antibiotics, and a negative HIV test 14 months ago; not pregnant and using an intrauterine device for contraception.

Vaginal pH measured 5.4. Saline wet-mount microscopy shows numerous polymorphonuclear leukocytes and motile, pear-shaped organisms with multiple flagella and a characteristic jerky movement; Gram stain shows many neutrophils but no predominant gram-positive yeast or clue cells.

Transvaginal ultrasound performed to exclude pelvic inflammatory complications demonstrates a normal-sized uterus and ovaries without free fluid; a small amount of echogenic material is visible within the vaginal canal, consistent with the discharge seen on examination.

Single-dose oral 2 g metronidazole given to the patient and partner produced marked symptomatic improvement within 48 hours; repeat wet mount at 1 week shows no motile organisms and NAAT returned negative.

explanation: The combination of frothy, malodorous yellow-green discharge, cervical punctate erythema, elevated vaginal pH, and demonstration of motile, flagellated protozoa on saline wet mount is diagnostic. Rapid clinical and microscopic cure after a single 2 g oral nitroimidazole dose confirms infection by Trichomonas vaginalis; partner treatment is essential to prevent reinfection.

hints.text: A 7-year-old boy brought to a rural outpatient clinic with 3 months of intermittent crampy abdominal pain and increasing episodes of loose stools containing fresh blood and mucus. Mother reports reduced appetite and that he has fallen behind classmates in growth; earlier this week he had a brief episode of partial rectal prolapse after straining at stool. On exam he is afebrile, pale, weight-for-age below the 5th centile and a small prolapse of rectal mucosa is visible while he coughs.

He lives in a subsistence-farming village, plays frequently in irrigated fields and around household latrines, usually barefoot. No routine deworming programme at school; immunisations up to date. No history of travel beyond the district, no chronic illness, and no recent antibiotic use.

Full blood count: haemoglobin 85 g/L, mean corpuscular volume 68 fL, white cell count 11.2 × 10^9/L with eosinophils 2.0 × 10^9/L. C-reactive protein 3 mg/L. Stool guaiac test positive. Faecal concentration microscopy requested.

Flexible sigmoidoscopy performed because of recurrent bleeding shows multiple slender organisms attached to the mucosa of the rectum and distal colon: thin anterior portions embedded in mucosa with thicker posterior segments trailing into the lumen; adjacent mucosa is erythematous with small superficial areas of bleeding.
Concentrated stool microscopy from this patient demonstrates numerous brown, barrel-shaped ova with thick shells and prominent bipolar plugs at each pole.

explanation: The clinical picture—school-age child from an area without routine deworming, chronic bloody diarrhoea with iron-deficiency anaemia and rectal prolapse—together with colonoscopic visualization of thin anterior worms embedded in the colonic mucosa and stool ova that are brown, barrel-shaped with bipolar plugs, identifies Trichuris trichiura infection. Management is anthelmintic therapy (for example mebendazole or albendazole per local guidelines) and addressing iron deficiency and sanitation to prevent reinfection.

hints.text: A 28-year-old man presents to the emergency clinic with a 3-week history of intermittent high fevers, progressively severe frontal headache, increasing daytime somnolence and recent confusion. He returned 10 days ago from a 6-week volunteer placement in rural northern Uganda. On examination he is febrile (38.6 °C), somnolent but rousable, and has several firm, non-tender posterior cervical lymph nodes with the largest measuring about 3 cm.

Illness began after a painful insect bite on the forearm that left a single erythematous papule. During the first two weeks he had cyclical fevers every 2–3 days and malaise; over the following week family noted personality change, daytime sleepiness and intermittent disorientation. He is otherwise healthy, HIV test last year was negative, and he took no chronic medications.

Blood tests: haemoglobin 110 g/L, WBC 6.2 × 10^9/L, platelets 180 × 10^9/L, CRP 35 mg/L. Thin blood film and wet preparation (Giemsa) performed on admission show motile, extracellular slender organisms with an undulating membrane and a single flagellum. Lumbar puncture: CSF WBC 120 × 10^6/L (predominantly lymphocytes), protein 1.1 g/L, glucose 3.8 mmol/L.

Brain MRI with contrast demonstrates patchy T2/FLAIR hyperintensity within the basal ganglia and periventricular white matter with mild diffuse meningeal enhancement, without mass lesion or focal abscess.

While in Uganda he spent most days working near riverine vegetation and repeatedly reported being bitten during daylight by a large, day‑biting, blood‑sucking fly that rested on both cattle and people (locally called the tsetse fly). Local clinics had reported several recent patients with similar fevers followed by progressive sleepiness.

explanation: Clinical picture of a traveller from sub‑Saharan Africa with an initial painful bite, posterior cervical lymphadenopathy, relapsing fevers, progression to meningoencephalitic symptoms, demonstration of motile extracellular flagellated organisms on Giemsa blood film, CSF lymphocytic pleocytosis, and exposure to tsetse fly is diagnostic of infection by Trypanosoma brucei. Diagnosis is confirmed by microscopy of blood or chancre aspirate, CSF exam for CNS involvement, and can be supported by PCR or specific serology; treatment choices depend on stage (haemolymphatic versus CNS) and include stage‑appropriate antitrypanosomal agents.

hints.text: A 68-year-old woman presents to clinic with 48 hours of severe burning pain across the left anterior chest, followed by development of grouped clear vesicles on an erythematous base confined to a single horizontal band beneath the left clavicle. Lesions are at slightly different stages (some intact vesicles, some crusting). She has type 2 diabetes treated with metformin and reports pain rated 8/10 interfering with sleep.

She recalls a childhood febrile illness with a generalized vesicular rash but has no record of any adult immunization against that childhood virus. No recent systemic corticosteroid use, chemotherapy, or known HIV risk factors. Her diabetes is otherwise well controlled, last HbA1c 52 mmol/mol (6.9%).

Routine bloods: white cell count 7.4 × 10^9/L, lymphocytes 1.2 × 10^9/L, CRP 5 mg/L. A bedside smear of fluid from a vesicle shows multinucleated giant epithelial cells; a swab for viral PCR has been sent.

Frontal chest radiograph obtained because of chest-wall location of the rash shows no consolidation or rib abnormality. Clinical photograph (attached to the chart) documents clustered vesicles in a unilateral thoracic dermatome without midline crossing.

The patient reports her 3-year-old grandchild visited 10–14 days earlier and had a recent illness characterized by fever and a widespread vesicular rash; the grandchild was diagnosed by his paediatrician as a primary generalized vesicular infection. The patient's lesion PCR returned positive for an enveloped, double-stranded DNA virus of the Herpesviridae, alpha subfamily, identical by sequencing to the virus cultured from the child's lesions.

explanation: Presentation is classic for reactivation of Varicella‑zoster virus: unilateral, dermatomal clustered vesicles preceded by neuropathic pain in an older adult with history of primary childhood infection. Tzanck smear showing multinucleated giant cells supports a herpesvirus; PCR and sequencing confirming an alpha‑herpesvirus identical to the child's isolate establishes that the same virus caused both the child's primary illness and the patient's dermatomal reactivation. First-line management is prompt antiviral therapy (e.g., oral valaciclovir or aciclovir started within 72 hours of rash onset) plus analgesia; early treatment reduces acute complications and may lower the risk of prolonged postherpetic neuralgia.

hints.text: A 24-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with 10 hours of profuse watery diarrhoea and several episodes of vomiting. She estimates passing large volumes of very watery stool and feels light-headed on standing; on arrival she is tachycardic at 120/min, blood pressure 86/52 mmHg, afebrile, with dry mucous membranes and decreased skin turgor.

Previously healthy, non-smoker, not taking regular medications and not pregnant. Symptoms began abruptly while she was working in a makeshift relief camp after storms; she tried oral rehydration with plain water at home but stool output continued at high volume. No abdominal pain beyond cramping, no visible blood in stools, and no chronic bowel disease.

Serum electrolytes show Na+ 125 mmol/L, K+ 2.7 mmol/L, HCO3− 14 mmol/L; creatinine 220 μmol/L; white cell count 8.8 × 10^9/L. Wet-mount stool exam demonstrates very numerous motile, comma-shaped organisms and abundant mucus but no red cells.

Point-of-care ultrasound performed for volume assessment shows a hyperdynamic left ventricle and a small, collapsible inferior vena cava (<1 cm). Abdominal plain radiograph is without dilatation or free air.

She reports drinking untreated well and river water after local flooding at the camp and notes several neighbours developed the same watery diarrhoea. The patient describes her stools as thin, grey, with flecks of mucus—patients at the camp called them 'rice-water' stools. After rapid intravenous crystalloid resuscitation she improved; a public-health team investigating a waterborne outbreak was notified.

explanation: The presentation of sudden, massive, painless watery diarrhoea with 'rice-water' stools after ingestion of contaminated water, rapid intravascular volume depletion with hyponatraemia and hypokalaemia, and many motile, comma-shaped organisms on stool wet mount is most consistent with infection by Vibrio cholerae. Immediate management is aggressive volume repletion; single-dose oral antibiotics can reduce stool volume and duration and public-health measures to secure water are essential.

hints.text: A 24-year-old woman presented to the emergency department with 72 hours of crampy abdominal pain and watery diarrhoea. Pain began periumbilically and over 48 hours localised to the right lower quadrant; she reported fever and two episodes of loose stools with small amounts of fresh blood. On examination she was febrile (38.1 °C), heart rate 96/min, with focal tenderness and mild guarding in the right lower quadrant but no palpable mass.

She was previously healthy, took no regular medications, and had no recent antibiotics. Symptoms progressed over three days from diffuse cramps to localized right lower‑quadrant pain and ongoing diarrhoea; she denied urinary symptoms, recent travel outside the region, or high‑risk sexual exposures. Menstrual history was unremarkable and pregnancy test was negative in the ED.

Blood testing showed leukocytosis 12.8 × 10^9/L with neutrophil predominance and C‑reactive protein 48 mg/L. Serum electrolytes were within reference limits. Stool microscopy demonstrated fecal leukocytes and occult blood positive; routine stool culture was submitted. Blood cultures remained negative.

Contrast CT of the abdomen and pelvis demonstrated thickening of the terminal ileum with several enlarged mesenteric lymph nodes adjacent to the ileocecal region; the appendix appeared normal. Findings were reported as terminal ileitis with mesenteric adenitis rather than appendicitis.

On directed questioning she recalled eating undercooked pork sausages at a family market five days before symptom onset; two relatives who ate the same food had brief febrile diarrhoea. No contact with farm animals was reported beyond that meal.

explanation: This young adult with febrile diarrhoea, right lower‑quadrant pain, CT evidence of terminal ileitis with mesenteric adenitis, and a recent meal of undercooked pork is most consistent with infection due to Yersinia enterocolitica. The pathogen commonly causes postprandial febrile enteritis with ileal involvement and mesenteric lymphadenitis, and is frequently linked to pork products and unpasteurised pork exposures. Diagnosis is confirmed by stool culture or selective cold‑enrichment techniques; treatment for symptomatic extraintestinal or severe cases includes appropriate antibiotics, while most uncomplicated gastroenteritis is managed supportively.

hints.text: A 29-year-old woman presents to the eye clinic with 10 days of progressively worsening left ocular pain, severe photophobia and decreased vision. She is a soft contact-lens wearer. On examination visual acuity in the left eye is 6/36, the cornea shows an epithelial defect with diffuse stromal haze and prominent conjunctival injection; there is disproportionate pain compared with apparent discharge.

She reports nightly contact-lens wear and occasional overnight use of monthly disposable lenses. Two weeks before symptoms she had been started on topical corticosteroid drops by her primary care physician for presumed allergic conjunctivitis; she completed four days of the steroid before pain worsened. She denies diabetes or systemic immunosuppression.

Corneal scrapings sent at presentation: Gram stain and routine bacterial cultures showed no growth at 48 hours; PCR for herpes simplex virus on corneal swab was negative. Peripheral blood count: 8.9 × 10^9/L, C-reactive protein 3 mg/L. Empiric topical antibiotic therapy produced no clinical improvement after 72 hours.

Repeat slit-lamp examination demonstrates a faint, crescentic ring-shaped stromal infiltrate developing at the periphery with perineural (radial) stromal infiltrates on corneal nerves; anterior chamber remains quiet without hypopyon. In vivo corneal confocal microscopy (done because of nonresponse to antibiotics) identified small (10–20 μm), highly reflective round structures within the stroma.

On detailed re-questioning she acknowledges rinsing lenses with tap water while traveling and swimming in a freshwater reservoir while wearing lenses three weeks earlier. Corneal culture plated on non-nutrient agar with a bacterial overlay subsequently produced motile trophozoite forms and double-walled cysts consistent with a free-living amoebic keratitis.

explanation: Contact-lens wear, recent topical steroid use, nonresponse to antibiotics, perineural infiltrates and a ring stromal lesion with corneal confocal microscopy showing reflective round cysts point to an amoebic keratitis. The exposure history (tap-water/lake contact while wearing lenses) and growth of motile trophozoites plus double-walled cysts on non-nutrient agar identify Acanthamoeba species as the causative pathogen.

hints.text: A 48-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with 6 days of fever to 39.2 °C, severe myalgias, frontal headache and progressive confusion. She reports poor oral intake and nausea but no vomiting or diarrhoea. She has no rash and denies focal respiratory or urinary symptoms. On examination she is febrile (39.0 °C), pulse 110/min, mildly disoriented to time, with otherwise non-focal neurologic exam and no obvious skin lesions.

Past medical history notable only for well-controlled type 2 diabetes managed with metformin. She works as a community gardener and spent substantial time clearing overgrown beds in late spring. She denies recent travel outside the region, has not received recent vaccinations, and reports that outpatient treatment with oral amoxicillin for presumed sinusitis over the prior week produced no improvement.

Initial bloodwork shows leukopenia with WBC 2.6 × 10^9/L and neutrophils 1.0 × 10^9/L, platelet count 68 × 10^9/L, haemoglobin 125 g/L. Serum transaminases are mildly elevated (AST 135 U/L, ALT 98 U/L). C-reactive protein 118 mg/L. Two sets of routine blood cultures were drawn and remained sterile at 48 hours.

Non-contrast CT head performed for acute confusion shows no acute intracranial process. Chest radiograph is clear without focal consolidation or pleural effusion. Abdominal ultrasound shows no hepatosplenomegaly and no focal fluid collections.

On re-evaluation the patient recalls removing an engorged tick from her anterior thigh after working in a wooded community lot about 9 days before symptom onset; she did not note an expanding erythematous lesion. A peripheral blood buffy-coat preparation demonstrated intracytoplasmic inclusions within neutrophils (morulae), and a whole-blood PCR assay returned positive for a granulocyte-tropic obligate intracellular bacterium.

explanation: The combination of recent tick exposure in a wooded area, febrile illness with headache and confusion, leukopenia (neutropenia), thrombocytopenia, mild transaminitis, and demonstration of morulae within neutrophils is most consistent with infection by Anaplasma phagocytophilum. Key discriminators from other tick-borne infections include the granulocytic (neutrophil) morulae (rather than monocytic inclusions), absence of an expanding erythematous migratory lesion, and the cytopenias with sterile blood cultures. Confirmation is by PCR on whole blood or by peripheral smear and serology; doxycycline is the recommended empiric therapy pending confirmation.

hints.text: A 52-year-old woman presented to the emergency department with 9 days of progressive left lower abdominal pain, fevers and anorexia. Pain began as dull discomfort after a bout of constipation and progressed to constant, severe pain with a palpable, tender mass in the left lower quadrant. On arrival she was febrile to 38.8°C, heart rate 102/min, blood pressure 118/74 mmHg; abdominal exam showed focal guarding and a 5-cm tender, fluctuant fullness in the left lower quadrant.

Two weeks earlier she had an episode diagnosed as uncomplicated sigmoid diverticulitis treated in clinic with oral ciprofloxacin alone for five days. Medical history notable for type 2 diabetes (HbA1c 6.8%), obesity (BMI 34 kg/m2) and no chronic immunosuppression. No recent travel, no animal exposures, no intravenous drug use.

Blood tests: WBC 15.6 × 10^9/L (neutrophils 12.0 × 10^9/L), CRP 182 mg/L. Two sets of blood cultures remained sterile after 5 days. CT-guided aspiration of the collection yielded 30 mL of foul-smelling purulent fluid; Gram stain showed numerous neutrophils and pleomorphic gram-negative bacilli. After 48 hours, anaerobic culture produced colonies of a non-lactose-fermenting gram-negative bacillus that hydrolysed esculin and grew on bile-containing medium.

Contrast-enhanced CT abdomen: a 6.2-cm rim-enhancing, loculated fluid collection adjacent to the sigmoid colon with pericolic fat stranding and several small intralesional gas locules; multiple sigmoid diverticula were present. No diffuse free intraperitoneal air to suggest a large perforation.

Patient was started empirically on IV ceftriaxone and fluids with percutaneous drain left in place; persistent fevers prompted review of the aspirate susceptibilities, which showed beta-lactamase–mediated resistance to penicillin. Therapy was changed to IV metronidazole while drainage continued; she defervesced within 48 hours, WBC and CRP trended down, and the collection resolved without operative intervention.

explanation: The clinical picture is an intra-abdominal abscess complicating recent diverticulitis with purulent fluid containing an anaerobic, bile-tolerant, esculin-hydrolysing gram-negative bacillus and beta-lactamase activity, with rapid clinical response to metronidazole plus drainage. These features are most consistent with Bacteroides fragilis as the causative pathogen.

hints.text: A 34-year-old woman presents to the emergency clinic 48 hours after symptom onset with abrupt fever to 39.3 °C, severe symmetric pain of wrists, metacarpophalangeal joints and ankles that prevents her from writing or walking unassisted, and a diffuse, blanching maculopapular rash across the trunk. She reports marked morning stiffness and says analgesics (paracetamol and ibuprofen) have provided minimal relief.

She is generally healthy, takes no chronic medications, has no prior diagnosis of inflammatory arthritis, is not pregnant and has no recent vaccinations. Symptoms began abruptly after a 7-day trip abroad; she denies localised joint trauma, oral ulcers, urethral symptoms, or bloody diarrhoea. There were no sick household contacts.

Initial blood tests: leukocytes 3.2 × 10^9/L with lymphocytes 0.7 × 10^9/L, haemoglobin 132 g/L, platelets 150 × 10^9/L, CRP 92 mg/L, ALT 85 U/L. Blood cultures drawn in ED showed no growth at 48 hours. A rapid antigen test for one common flavivirus was negative on presentation.

Point-of-care musculoskeletal ultrasound of the right wrist and ankle demonstrates synovial thickening with small joint effusions and tenosynovitis of extensor tendons; plain radiographs show no erosive changes.

She returned five days before presentation from relatives in a Caribbean coastal town where she had numerous daytime mosquito bites from Aedes species; local health authorities had issued alerts for an outbreak of an acute febrile illness marked by incapacitating symmetric joint pain. Acute serum sent on day 3 of illness was positive by PCR at the regional reference lab for the outbreak arbovirus.

explanation: The constellation of sudden high fever, severe incapacitating symmetric peripheral arthralgia with synovitis and rash after recent travel to an area with Aedes mosquito transmission, together with early leukopenia, modest thrombocytopenia and elevated transaminases, is most consistent with infection by Chikungunya virus. PCR positivity on an acute specimen confirms infection during the viremic phase; the prominent, often debilitating joint pain with ultrasound evidence of synovitis helps distinguish this from other arboviral and bacterial causes of febrile arthritis.

hints.text: A 37-year-old male high‑school teacher presents with a 3‑week history of progressively dry cough, sore throat and hoarseness. He describes intermittent wheeze and chest tightness but no pleuritic pain; low‑grade fevers to 38.0 °C were noted during the first week. He uses inhaled budesonide for mild asthma. On examination he is comfortable at rest, T 37.6 °C, pulse 92/min, RR 18/min, SpO2 96% on room air, with scattered expiratory wheeze and diffuse rhonchi but no focal bronchial consolidation.

Symptoms began with pharyngitis and malaise and evolved over days to persistent cough. Two weeks into illness his general practitioner prescribed a 5‑day course of oral amoxicillin without clinical improvement. He has no recent travel, is a non‑smoker, takes no systemic immunosuppressants, and reports several household contacts with milder cough over the same period.

Bloods: WBC 8.2 × 10^9/L, neutrophils 5.4 × 10^9/L; CRP 18 mg/L. Sputum Gram stain: predominantly epithelial cells, no predominant bacterial morphotype; blood cultures sterile. Nasopharyngeal PCR returned positive for a small obligate intracellular bacterium; paired acute and convalescent sera showed a fourfold rise in specific IgG directed at the same agent. Cold agglutinin titre 1:32.

Chest radiograph shows patchy, peribronchial and perihilar reticulonodular opacities with mild focal air‑space shadowing in the right lower zone but no dense lobar consolidation. Low‑dose CT of the thorax (performed to clarify extent) demonstrates bronchial wall thickening and multifocal patchy ground‑glass change, greatest centrally and in the right lower lobe.

Public‑health follow‑up revealed 18 students and 3 staff at his school reported similar prolonged cough/pharyngitis over the preceding 8 weeks. The regional laboratory reported that PCR from three pupil specimens detected the same small, gram‑negative obligate intracellular bacterium; the organism cannot be propagated on routine bacterial media and requires eukaryotic cell culture for isolation. The school cluster and the patient’s positive PCR were concordant.

explanation: Presentation of a subacute, non‑lobar lower respiratory infection with normal white count, minimal inflammatory marker elevation, poor response to a beta‑lactam, positive PCR for an obligate intracellular organism, serologic rise and a school outbreak that required cell‑culture for isolation points to Chlamydophila pneumoniae. Diagnosis was made by nucleic acid detection and paired serology; recommended therapy is with a macrolide or tetracycline rather than a beta‑lactam because of the organism’s intracellular lifecycle and limited susceptibility to beta‑lactams.

hints.text: A 46-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with 7 days of progressive fevers, frontal headache, myalgias and a dry, non-productive cough. She reports increasing shortness of breath over 48 hours. On arrival she is febrile to 39.2 °C, pulse 92/min, respiratory rate 22/min and oxygen saturation 93% on room air. Examination shows diminished breath sounds and fine crackles at the right lower lung field; no skin rash or lymphadenopathy.

Medical history notable only for well-controlled type 2 diabetes managed with metformin. She saw her GP four days into symptoms and was prescribed a 5-day course of an oral β-lactam antibiotic; symptoms progressed despite treatment. No recent hospitalisations, no known immunosuppression, and she is up to date with routine vaccinations.

Blood tests: white blood cell count 9.8 × 10^9/L with neutrophils 7.5 × 10^9/L, C‑reactive protein 120 mg/L, ALT 95 U/L, AST 88 U/L. Blood cultures drawn on admission are pending; urinary antigen tests for two common atypical pathogens are negative.

Chest radiograph shows a dense lobar consolidation in the right lower zone with small blunting of the right costophrenic angle. A contrast CT thorax (performed to exclude pulmonary embolism given her hypoxaemia) confirms focal right lower-lobe consolidation and a small pleural effusion; no cavitation or diffuse bilateral interstitial pattern.

On further questioning she reports buying a second‑hand pet parrot and cleaning its cage about 10 days before symptom onset. The bird had been lethargic and several birds at the store had recently died.

explanation: This patient has an atypical community‑acquired pneumonia with systemic features and mild transaminase elevation, failed a β‑lactam, and has a clear recent exposure to an ill parrot—an exposure that points to an avian-origin intracellular respiratory pathogen. The combination of single‑lobe consolidation, elevated transaminases, negative routine urinary antigen tests, and bird exposure supports infection with Chlamydophila psittaci. Diagnostic confirmation is by PCR on respiratory specimens or a rising specific antibody titre; tetracyclines (doxycycline) are the treatment of choice and clinical improvement after targeted therapy is expected.

hints.text: A 42-year-old man presents with 10 days of fever (maximum 39.2 °C), severe frontal headache, progressive malaise and a dry cough. He describes pleuritic right-sided chest pain and mild right upper-quadrant discomfort. On examination he is tachycardic (110/min), respiratory rate 20/min, oxygen saturation 96% on room air; auscultation reveals reduced breath sounds and crackles at the right base without focal neurologic deficit or rash.

He is an otherwise healthy office worker, non-smoker, not on regular medication, no known immunosuppression, and no prosthetic valves. His GP started oral amoxicillin 4 days before hospital presentation with no clinical improvement. He reports no recent overseas travel for the past year and no routine occupational animal contact.

Blood tests show white cell count 6.4 × 10^9/L, neutrophils 4.2 × 10^9/L, C-reactive protein 112 mg/L, ALT 132 U/L, AST 98 U/L, alkaline phosphatase 140 U/L. Two sets of blood cultures remain sterile at 48 hours. Urinary antigen testing for Legionella species is negative, and nasopharyngeal PCR for influenza and SARS-CoV-2 are negative.

Chest radiograph demonstrates patchy consolidation of the right lower lobe. Contrast CT chest confirms focal lobar consolidation in the right lower lobe with a small adjacent pleural effusion, without cavitation, lymphadenopathy, or diffuse interstitial change.

When asked again about recent events, he reports attending a rural agricultural festival two weeks before symptom onset and assisting with a live demonstration of sheep and goat birthing; he recalls handling afterbirth material and sweeping dry, dusty stalls. Several other festival volunteers later reported febrile illnesses.

explanation: The combination of subacute febrile illness with atypical pneumonia, modest leukocytosis but marked inflammatory markers and transaminase elevation, sterile blood cultures, poor response to a beta-lactam, and a recent exposure to parturient small ruminants (aerosolized birthing products) strongly suggests infection with Coxiella burnetii. Diagnosis is made with targeted serology (rising phase II antibodies) or PCR on appropriate specimens; first-line therapy for acute disease is doxycycline.

hints.text: A 34-year-old woman presents to urgent care with 14 days of watery diarrhoea: 6–10 voluminous, non-bloody stools per day with nocturnal episodes, crampy lower abdominal pain and a 3 kg weight loss. She is afebrile, pulse 88/min, blood pressure 112/72 mmHg; exam shows dry mucous membranes and mild diffuse abdominal tenderness without guarding.

Previously healthy, no chronic gastrointestinal disease, HIV test negative two months ago, not on immunosuppressants. She received a 5-day course of ciprofloxacin while abroad for presumed bacterial traveller’s diarrhoea but symptoms persisted and waxed and waned over the subsequent two weeks. No recent hospitalisations or antibiotic-associated diarrhoea before the current illness.

Bloods: haemoglobin 132 g/L, white cell count 7.2 × 10^9/L, CRP 12 mg/L, potassium 3.2 mmol/L. Stool studies: routine bacterial culture and routine ova-and-parasite wet mount were negative; concentrated stool with modified acid-fast stain demonstrated variably staining, spherical oocysts approximately 8–10 μm in diameter that exhibited bright autofluorescence under UV microscopy.

Contrast-enhanced CT abdomen/pelvis performed to exclude complication: mild, diffuse small-bowel and colonic wall thickening consistent with enteritis; no obstruction, no abscess, no mesenteric stranding.

Public-health follow-up revealed attendance at a city food festival 10–14 days before symptom onset; several other attendees who ate multiple servings of fresh imported raspberries from a single vendor reported similarly prolonged watery diarrhoea. The patient recalls consuming raspberry-topped desserts and a raspberry smoothie at that vendor.

explanation: Prolonged watery diarrhoea in an immunocompetent adult after probable exposure to imported fresh raspberries, combined with demonstration of 8–10 μm variably acid-fast oocysts that autofluoresce, is most consistent with infection by Cyclospora cayetanensis. Diagnosis is made by identification of the oocysts (or by PCR); treatment of choice is trimethoprim–sulfamethoxazole.

hints.text: A 34-year-old man presents to an urban infectious-disease clinic with four weeks of profuse watery diarrhoea — 6–8 large-volume, non-bloody stools daily with nocturnal urgency, crampy periumbilical pain and an unintentional 6 kg weight loss. He reports intermittent low-grade fevers and lightheadedness when standing. On exam he is afebrile to touch, pulse 110/min, mucous membranes dry and temporally wasted; abdominal exam is soft with mild diffuse tenderness but no peritonism.

Known HIV-positive, diagnosed 8 months earlier; antiretroviral therapy has been intermittent because of access and adherence issues, last clinic visit 4 months ago. He declined pneumocystis prophylaxis with trimethoprim–sulfamethoxazole when first offered. A CD4 count obtained 3 weeks before presentation was 80 × 10^6/L. No recent hospitalizations or broad‑spectrum antibiotics.

Blood tests: haemoglobin 112 g/L, leucocyte count 5.8 × 10^9/L, CRP 22 mg/L, Na+ 130 mmol/L, K+ 3.4 mmol/L. Stool: three separate routine wet-mount ova-and-cyst examinations were reported negative and bacterial stool culture was sterile; antigen testing for a common intestinal protozoan and multiplex PCR panel were nondiagnostic.

Contrast-enhanced abdominal CT showed segmental circumferential thickening of the jejunum and ileum with mild mesenteric lymphadenopathy and a small volume of free pelvic fluid; the colon and terminal ileum were largely unremarkable on imaging.

After five days of empiric ciprofloxacin with no clinical change, high-dose trimethoprim–sulfamethoxazole was started.
