Early empiric doxycycline is indicated when this syndrome is suspected because delays in therapy increase morbidity.

hints.text: A 76-year-old man on an orthopaedic ward, day 12 after open reduction and internal fixation of a femoral neck fracture, develops 36 hours of fever, rigors, dysuria and new confusion. Temperature is 38.9°C, blood pressure 95/58 mmHg, heart rate 110 bpm. A Foley catheter has been in place since surgery. Suprapubic tenderness is present on examination.

Medical history notable for type 2 diabetes and chronic kidney disease stage 3. Hospital course complicated by hospital-acquired pneumonia treated with ceftriaxone then piperacillin-tazobactam from postoperative day 4 to 9. Indwelling urinary catheter was left for postoperative urinary retention. No recent travel or animal exposures.

WBC 15.8 × 10^9/L, CRP 220 mg/L, creatinine 150 µmol/L. Urinalysis: leukocyte esterase positive, >100 leucocytes per high-power field, nitrite negative. Two sets of blood cultures and a urine culture grew catalase-negative Gram-positive cocci in short chains and pairs. Isolates hydrolysed esculin in bile and grew in 6.5% NaCl. Antimicrobial testing showed resistance to ceftriaxone and ampicillin, retained susceptibility to linezolid.

Contrast CT abdomen and pelvis demonstrates left-sided pyelonephritis with cortical striated nephrogram changes and perinephric fat stranding. No drainable renal or perinephric abscess and no evidence of hardware or bone infection near the orthopaedic site.

Empiric vancomycin plus piperacillin-tazobactam were started. Blood cultures remained positive after 48 hours. Laboratory reported vancomycin MIC >32 mg/L for the blood isolate. Foley catheter was removed and antimicrobial therapy changed to linezolid, after which blood cultures cleared within 72 hours and the patient defervesced.

explanation: The combination of hospital-acquired urinary source with an indwelling catheter, catalase-negative Gram-positive cocci in short chains that hydrolyse esculin and grow in 6.5% NaCl, plus ampicillin resistance and a very high vancomycin MIC that correlated with persistence on vancomycin, identifies a vancomycin-resistant enterococcal bloodstream infection. Ampicillin resistance makes Enterococcus faecium the more likely species in this setting. Clinical improvement following catheter removal and linezolid supports that organism as the pathogen.

hints.text: A 38-year-old woman presents to hepatology with an 8-week history of dull right upper-quadrant pain, intermittent low-grade fevers, progressive pruritus, and a 4 kg weight loss. She noticed scleral icterus for the past 10 days. Examination shows a tender liver edge 3 cm below the right costal margin and jaundice of the sclerae.

She has no chronic liver disease, takes no regular medications, and is not immunosuppressed. She underwent laparoscopic cholecystectomy five years ago. Over the prior two months she had two brief admissions for presumed biliary sepsis treated with intravenous antibiotics, with only transient symptom relief.

Peripheral blood count shows WBC 15.6 × 10^9/L with eosinophils 3.8 × 10^9/L. Total bilirubin 46 µmol/L. ALP 710 U/L, GGT 520 U/L, ALT 89 U/L. CRP 22 mg/L. Blood cultures are negative. Two separate stool examinations for ova and parasites returned negative.

Contrast CT of the abdomen demonstrates multiple serpiginous low-attenuation tracts extending from the subcapsular surface into the right hepatic lobe and periportal region. MRCP shows mild intrahepatic biliary dilatation and a 12 mm elongated intraductal filling defect that appears partly mobile rather than a discrete stone.

On targeted reinterview she reports helping at a family smallholding three months earlier. While there she washed and frequently ate raw watercress harvested from a slow-moving stream that ran through pasture grazed by sheep and goats. Her first systemic symptoms began about five weeks after that exposure.

explanation: The combination of a delayed onset after ingestion of raw freshwater plants from sheep-grazed areas, prominent eosinophilia, migrating serpiginous hepatic tracts on imaging, and later biliary obstruction is most consistent with infection by Fasciola hepatica. The parasite migrates through the hepatic parenchyma during an acute hepatic phase causing eosinophilia and subcapsular tracts, then the biliary phase produces cholestatic liver tests and intraductal filling defects. Stool examination often remains negative early because eggs appear months after infection. Diagnosis is confirmed by serology or later demonstration of large operculated eggs in stool or by endoscopic retrieval of flukes. Treatment of choice is a nitrothiazol derivative effective against adult and immature flukes.
